# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a synchronous and an asynchronous counter. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **synchronous counter** has all flip-flops triggered by the *same* clock signal, with combinational next-state logic deciding which FFs toggle. An **asynchronous (ripple) counter** has each FF clocked by the *output* of the previous FF, causing the toggling to propagate (ripple) through the chain.

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Clock | Common to all FFs | Cascaded through FFs |
| Speed | Faster, independent of stages | Slower, scales with $n$ |
| Glitches | Minimal | Significant during transition |
| Hardware | More combinational logic | Simpler wiring |

**Advantage of synchronous:** Higher operating frequency, no cumulative ripple delay.
**Advantage of asynchronous:** Simpler hardware, fewer gates, suitable for low-frequency dividers.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a sequence generator? Write its Verilog modelling styles.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sequence generator** is a sequential circuit that produces a predetermined bit-stream on its output, one bit per clock cycle. It is implemented as a Moore or Mealy finite state machine.

**Verilog modelling styles:**
1. **Behavioural style** — using `always @(posedge clk)` blocks with a state register and `case` statement.
2. **Structural style** — instantiating flip-flops and combinational gates explicitly.
3. **Shift-register style** — using a left/right shift register with feedback combinational logic.

The most KTU-preferred style is **behavioural** because it is HDL-portable and synthesis-friendly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an *internal choice* in Part B. Both options below carry identical marks and sub-part distribution.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Design a **4-bit synchronous BCD counter** with synchronous active-high reset. Provide:
- (i) the state transition table, [3 marks]
- (ii) the next-state Boolean equations, [2 marks]
- (iii) the complete Verilog behavioural model. [2 marks]

**(b)** Design a **4-bit asynchronous (ripple) up counter** using Verilog structural modelling. Provide:
- (i) the timing analysis showing ripple delay, [3 marks]
- (ii) the Verilog code using D flip-flop primitives, [2 marks]
- (iii) a comparison of maximum operating frequency with the synchronous version, given $t_{pd} = 25$ ns per FF, $t_{cq} = 2$ ns, $t_{su} = 1$ ns, $t_{logic} = 5$ ns. [2 marks]

---

#### **Solution to A(a)**

**(i) State Transition Table (3 marks):**

| Present Q3 Q2 Q1 Q0 | Next Q3⁺ Q2⁺ Q1⁺ Q0⁺ |
|---|---|
| 0000 | 0001 |
| 0001 | 0010 |
| 0010 | 0011 |
| 0011 | 0100 |
| 0100 | 0101 |
| 0101 | 0110 |
| 0110 | 0111 |
| 0111 | 1000 |
| 1000 | 1001 |
| 1001 | 0000 |

**(ii) Next-State Boolean Equations (2 marks):**

The simplest approach is to use an *adder* with a *gate-based reset*:
$$
Q^{+} = (Q + 1) \cdot \overline{(Q = 9)}
$$

Equivalently, on detecting state 1001, force the next state to 0000:
$$
Q^+ = \begin{cases} Q + 1 & \text{if } Q \ne 9 \\ 0 & \text{if } Q = 9 \end{cases}
$$

**(iii) Verilog Behavioural Model (2 marks):**

```verilog
module bcd_counter_sync (q, clk, reset);
  output [3:0] q;
  input        clk, reset;
  reg [3:0]   q;

  always @(posedge clk) begin
    if (reset)
      q <= 4'b0000;
    else if (q == 4'd9)
      q <= 4'd0;
    else
      q <= q + 1;
  end
endmodule
```

---

#### **Solution to A(b)**

**(i) Timing Analysis (3 marks):**

For a 4-bit ripple counter, the worst-case transition is **0111 → 1000** (all four FFs change state). The ripple delay is:

$$
t_{ripple} = n \cdot t_{pd} = 4 \cdot 25 \text{ ns} = 100 \text{ ns}
$$

**Glitch window:** The transient states during this transition are 0111 → 0110 → 0100 → 0000 → 1000. Any downstream combinational logic that samples the output during the 0–100 ns window will read *garbage*.

**(ii) Verilog Structural Code (2 marks):**

```verilog
module ripple_up_4bit (q, clk, reset);
  output [3:0] q;
  input        clk, reset;

  // T flip-flop from D flip-flop: D = Q ^ T
  // Cascading Q[i-1] into CLK of FF[i] creates the ripple.
  wire q0_w, q1_w, q2_w;

  dff_async FF0 (q[0], clk,   reset, 1'b1);
  dff_async FF1 (q[1], q[0],  reset, 1'b1);
  dff_async FF2 (q[2], q[1],  reset, 1'b1);
  dff_async FF3 (q[3], q[2],  reset, 1'b1);
endmodule
```

> **Tip:** Alternatively, the standard KTU-accepted form is the **behavioural** version with the comment that synthesis infers a ripple topology:
> ```verilog
> always @(posedge clk or posedge reset)
>   if (reset) q <= 4'b0000;
>   else       q <= q + 1;
> ```

**(iii) Maximum Frequency Comparison (2 marks):**

**Synchronous:**
$$
f_{max}^{sync} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su}} = \dfrac{1}{2 + 5 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{8 \text{ ns}} = 125 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Asynchronous (4-bit):**
$$
f_{max}^{async} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot t_{pd}} = \dfrac{1}{4 \cdot 25 \text{ ns}} = \dfrac{1}{100 \text{ ns}} = 10 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Comparison:** The synchronous version is **12.5× faster** than the ripple version. This gap widens as $n$ increases.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Implement a **Verilog sequence generator** that produces the repeating bit-stream **11010** using:
- (i) the **FSM (Moore machine)** approach, [4 marks]
- (ii) the **shift-register with feedback** approach. [3 marks]

**(b)** Write the Verilog code for a **synchronous 3-bit up/down counter** with:
- (i) asynchronous active-low reset, [2 marks]
- (ii) mode control input `up` (1 = count up, 0 = count down), [2 marks]
- (iii) a brief testbench demonstrating both modes. [3 marks]

---

#### **Solution to B(a)**

**(i) FSM (Moore) Approach (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — State Assignment (one-hot, 5 states for 5-bit sequence):**

| State | Q2 Q1 Q0 | Output (Moore) |
|---|---|---|
| S0 | 000 | 1 |
| S1 | 001 | 1 |
| S2 | 010 | 0 |
| S3 | 011 | 1 |
| S4 | 100 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Verilog FSM Model:**

```verilog
module seq_gen_moore_11010 (clk, reset, out);
  input        clk, reset;
  output       out;
  reg [2:0]   state, next;
  reg         out;

  parameter S0 = 3'b000, S1 = 3'b001,
            S2 = 3'b010, S3 = 3'b011, S4 = 3'b100;

  // State register
  always @(posedge clk or negedge reset)
    if (!reset) state <= S0;
    else        state <= next;

  // Next-state logic
  always @(*) begin
    case (state)
      S0: next = S1;
      S1: next = S2;
      S2: next = S3;
      S3: next = S4;
      S4: next = S0;
      default: next = S0;
    endcase
  end

  // Moore output
  always @(*) begin
    case (state)
      S0: out = 1;
      S1: out = 1;
      S2: out = 0;
      S3: out = 1;
      S4: out = 0;
      default: out = 0;
    endcase
  end
endmodule
```

**(ii) Shift-Register Approach (3 marks):**

For the sequence `11010` of length $L = 5$, a 3-bit shift register is sufficient. The feedback function is derived as:

$$
D = f(Q_2, Q_1, Q_0) = \overline{Q_2}\,\overline{Q_1}\,Q_0 \;+\; Q_2\,\overline{Q_0}
$$

```verilog
module seq_gen_sr_11010 (clk, reset, out);
  input  clk, reset;
  output out;
  reg [2:0] q;
  wire d;

  assign d = (~q[2] & ~q[1] & q[0]) | (q[2] & ~q[0]);
  assign out = q[0];

  always @(posedge clk or negedge reset)
    if (!reset) q <= 3'b110;       // initial state
    else        q <= {q[1:0], d};
endmodule
```

---

#### **Solution to B(b)**

**(i) & (ii) 3-bit Synchronous Up/Down Counter with Asynchronous Active-Low Reset (2 + 2 = 4 marks):**

```verilog
module up_down_counter_3bit (q, clk, reset_n, up);
  output [2:0] q;
  input        clk, reset_n, up;
  reg [2:0]   q;

  always @(posedge clk or negedge reset_n) begin
    if (!reset_n)
      q <= 3'b000;
    else if (up)
      q <= q + 1;
    else
      q <= q - 1;
  end
endmodule
```

> **KTU Note:** `reset_n` is **active-low** (note the underscore `_n` convention and the `negedge reset_n` sensitivity). The reset is *asynchronous* because it appears alongside `posedge clk` in the sensitivity list.

**(iii) Testbench Demonstrating Both Modes (3 marks):**

```verilog
module tb_up_down;
  reg        clk, reset_n, up;
  wire [2:0] q;

  up_down_counter_3bit uut (q, clk, reset_n, up);

  initial clk = 0;
  always #5 clk = ~clk;        // 100 MHz

  initial begin
    reset_n = 0; up = 0; #12;  // release reset, count down briefly
    reset_n = 1;
    up      = 0;
    #80;                       // count down for 80 ns
    up      = 1;
    #80;                       // count up for 80 ns
    $finish;
  end
endmodule
```

**Expected simulation behaviour:**

| Time | Mode | Sequence Observed |
|---|---|---|
| 0–12 ns | Reset (active-low) | q = 000 |
| 12–92 ns | Down (up=0) | 000 → 111 → 110 → 101 → 100 → 011 ... |
| 92–172 ns | Up (up=1) | continues upward through 100, 101, 110, 111, 000, 001 ... |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Using `=` (blocking) instead of `<=` (non-blocking) inside `always @(posedge clk)`.** This is a *fatal flaw* costing 2 marks per occurrence. The KTU model answer key explicitly penalises blocking assignment in sequential blocks.
> 2. **Forgetting to list all flip-flops in the async-reset sensitivity list.** `always @(posedge clk or posedge reset)` must include *all* asynchronous signals. Omitting `reset` from the list converts an asynchronous reset into a synchronous one — and the examiner will deduct marks.
> 3. **Confusing Moore and Mealy sequence generator outputs.** Moore output is registered (synchronous, glitch-free); Mealy output is combinational (faster, but glitchy). State this clearly in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Off-by-one error in MOD counter design.** For a MOD-10 counter, the rollover condition is `q == 9` (not `q == 10`, which would never be reached in a 4-bit register). Examiners *love* testing this.
> 5. **Failing to provide the next-state Boolean equation** when asked for "design" rather than just "code." Code alone is worth only 2 of the 7 marks in a design sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 (Verilog Counters & Sequence Generators):**

- **Ripple counter:** Each FF is clocked by the previous FF's Q output. Ripple delay = $n \cdot t_{pd}$. Glitch window present. Lower hardware complexity.
- **Synchronous counter:** All FFs share one global clock. $f_{max}$ independent of $n$. Slightly more hardware (adder or PLA). No glitch window beyond one $t_{cq}$.
- **Verilog for sequential logic:** Always use **non-blocking assignment** (`<=`) inside `always @(posedge clk)`. This mimics real hardware parallel update.
- **Asynchronous reset:** Sensitivity list is `(posedge clk or posedge reset)`. Reset acts *immediately*, independent of clock.
- **Synchronous reset:** Sensitivity list is `(posedge clk)` only. Reset takes effect *only* on the next clock edge.
- **MOD-m counter:** Use `n = ceil(log2(m))` FFs. Detect terminal state and force rollover. Don't-care states are $2^n - m$ in number.
- **BCD counter:** Rollover when `q == 4'd9`. Verilog: `else if (q == 4'd9) q <= 4'd0;`
- **Up/Down counter:** Use a `mode` control input to gate between `q + 1` and `q - 1`.
- **Sequence generator — FSM (Moore) approach:** State register + `case` statement for next-state + output logic that depends *only* on state.
- **Sequence generator — Shift-register approach:** Use $n$-bit shift register with combinational feedback $D = f(Q_{n-1}, \dots, Q_0)$. Derive $f$ using Karnaugh map with don't-cares.
- **Mealy vs Moore:** Mealy = output depends on state *and* input (faster, glitchy). Moore = output depends *only* on state (slower by 1 cycle, glitch-free).
- **Testbench essentials:** `initial` block for stimulus, `always #5 clk = ~clk;` for clock generation, `$finish;` to terminate, `$monitor` or `$display` for tracing.
- **Synthesis vs simulation difference:** Behavioural `q <= q + 1;` simulates synchronously, but synthesis tool decides whether to infer a ripple or synchronous counter based on target architecture. Always *state* your intent in a comment.
- **KTU golden rule:** When the question says "design using Verilog", provide (1) state diagram or transition table, (2) Boolean equations, (3) Verilog code, (4) brief testbench or waveform description. Hurdle marks are awarded for all four.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Characteristics of TTL and CMOS Logic Families - Logic Levels and Noise Margins, Fan-out, Propagation Delay, Transition Time, Power Consumption and Power Delay Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIT205 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN (ELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrical Characteristics of TTL and CMOS Logic Families - Logic Levels and Noise Margins, Fan-out, Propagation Delay, Transition Time, Power Consumption and Power Delay Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Logic Families – The Foundation

A **Logic Family** is a group of digital integrated circuits (ICs) constructed using a consistent underlying technology (e.g., transistors, diodes, resistors) that share standardized electrical behavior, allowing them to be interconnected seamlessly.

The two dominant logic families we study here are:
- **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic):** Built using **Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs)** as the primary switching element. The sub-series we focus on is **74LS (Low-power Schottky)** which is the workhorse of legacy digital design.
- **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor):** Built using paired **NMOS and PMOS transistors**. Series: **74HC (High-speed CMOS)** and **74HCT (High-speed CMOS, TTL-compatible)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The exam questions on Module 4 almost always compare TTL vs. CMOS across the same metric. Memorize the *direction* of advantage for each parameter (e.g., CMOS wins on power, TTL wins on speed historically).

---

## 1.2 Logic Levels – The Language of 0 and 1

A digital signal does not exist as a perfect "0 V" or "5 V." Instead, it occupies a **range of voltages** that the receiver gate interprets as either LOW or HIGH. These are defined by **four threshold voltages**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum input voltage recognized as LOW | $V_{IL(\max)}$ | Worst-case LOW input the driver must produce |
| Maximum input voltage recognized as HIGH | $V_{IH(\min)}$ | Worst-case HIGH input the driver must produce |
| Minimum output voltage guaranteed in LOW state | $V_{OL(\max)}$ | Worst-case LOW output the driver provides |
| Minimum output voltage guaranteed in HIGH state | $V_{OH(\min)}$ | Worst-case HIGH output the driver provides |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition – The Two-Doorway Analogy:**
> Imagine a corridor with **two doorways**. A LOW signal is anything that fits *under the LOW doorway ceiling* ($V_{IL}$), and a HIGH signal is anything that fits *over the HIGH doorway floor* ($V_{IH}$). The region *between* the two doorways (roughly 0.8 V to 2.0 V for TTL) is the **forbidden zone** — no logic gate is allowed to settle here. If a signal lingers there, the gate behaves unpredictably and may oscillate or draw enormous current.

**Typical Values (V_CC = 5 V):**

| Parameter | TTL (74LS) | CMOS (74HC) |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{IL(\max)}$ | 0.8 V | 1.5 V |
| $V_{IH(\min)}$ | 2.0 V | 3.5 V |
| $V_{OL(\max)}$ | 0.5 V | 0.33 V |
| $V_{OH(\min)}$ | 2.7 V | 4.4 V |

---

## 1.3 Noise Margins – Immunity Against the Real World

Even in a perfect circuit, **electrical noise** (crosstalk, ground bounce, EMI, inductive spikes) corrupts signal voltages. A logic family must tolerate this noise without flipping a bit incorrectly.

**Noise Margin** is the *safety buffer* (measured in volts) between the driver's worst output and the receiver's worst input requirement.

- **Noise Margin HIGH:** $NM_H = V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)}$
- **Noise Margin LOW:** $NM_L = V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** A higher noise margin means the gate is more **robust** in noisy industrial environments (factories, motors, automotive systems). TTL has lower noise margins (~0.7 V) compared to CMOS (~1.0 V under HC). This is why CMOS dominated portable and noise-sensitive applications.

---

## 1.4 Fan-Out – How Many Friends Can One Output Drive?

**Fan-out (FO)** is the maximum number of *similar* logic gate inputs that a single output can reliably drive while maintaining guaranteed logic levels.

A driver output must:
1. **Sink** enough current in the LOW state (each input draws $I_{IL}$).
2. **Source** enough current in the HIGH state (each input draws $I_{IH}$).

$$FO_{LOW} = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OL(\max)}}{I_{IL(\max)}} \right\rfloor$$

$$FO_{HIGH} = \left\lfloor \frac{I_{OH(\max)}}{I_{IH(\max)}} \right\rfloor$$

The **worst-case (guaranteed) fan-out** is the smaller of these two.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a water pump (the driver). It can only push so many gallons per minute. Each connected sprinkler head (the load) demands a fixed flow. If you connect too many sprinklers, the pressure (voltage) drops below the level needed to "open" a sprinkler properly — that sprinkler receives a "weak HIGH" or "weak LOW."

---

## 1.5 Propagation Delay & Transition Time – How Fast Can It Switch?

- **Propagation Delay ($t_p$)**: The time between a 50% transition at the input and the corresponding 50% transition at the output. We distinguish $t_{pLH}$ (LOW-to-HIGH) and $t_{pHL}$ (HIGH-to-LOW).
- **Average Propagation Delay:**

$$\bar{t_p} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

- **Transition Time ($t_t$)**: The time for the output to swing between the **10%** and **90%** voltage levels (rise time $t_r$ or fall time $t_f$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A BJT in TTL acts like a heavy freight train — it accelerates fast and decelerates fast but cannot change direction (logic state) instantly. A CMOS gate is like a sports car — slower to accelerate at sub-micron geometries due to gate capacitance, but at modern nodes (65 nm, 28 nm) it leaves TTL in the dust.

---

## 1.6 Power Consumption & Power-Delay Product – The Efficiency Scorecard

- **Static Power Dissipation ($P_S$):** Power consumed when the output is stable (no switching). CMOS has *near-zero* static power (only tiny leakage); TTL has substantial static power (~2–10 mW per gate).
- **Dynamic Power Dissipation ($P_D$):** Power consumed during switching due to charging/discharging load capacitance.

$$P_D = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

- **Total Power:** $P_{tot} = P_S + P_D$
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP) – Figure of Merit:**

$$\text{PDP} = P_{avg} \times \bar{t_p} \quad \text{[in Joules]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Figure of Merit:** PDP measures the *energy a gate consumes per switching event*. **Lower PDP = better gate.** Modern CMOS has PDPs in the **femtojoule range** ($10^{-15}$ J), which is why billions of transistors fit on a chip without melting it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Levels — Deeper Dive

### 2.1.1 Transfer Characteristics of TTL (74LS NAND)

The TTL NAND gate uses a **multi-emitter input transistor**. The Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC) shows three distinct regions:

1. **Cut-off region** ($V_{in} < 0.5$ V): Q1 emitter-base junctions are forward biased; output transistor Q3 is OFF; $V_{out} \approx V_{OH}$.
2. **Transition region** ($0.5\text{ V} < V_{in} < 1.3$ V): Q1 partially conducts; the output transitions sharply. The **gain** ($-dV_{out}/dV_{in}$) is very high.
3. **Saturation region** ($V_{in} > 1.3$ V): Q3 is ON and saturated; $V_{out} = V_{OL} \approx 0.2\text{–}0.4$ V.

The **small transition region width** is what gives TTL its *sharp* switching and explains why TTL has a low noise margin — the gate commits to a state very quickly with little ambiguity.

### 2.1.2 Transfer Characteristics of CMOS

CMOS gates use complementary pull-up (PMOS) and pull-down (NMOS) transistors. The VTC is virtually **symmetrical** around $V_{DD}/2$ and has an extremely **sharp** transition (theoretically infinite gain in the switching region). This symmetry is why noise margins for HIGH and LOW are nearly equal in CMOS.

### 2.1.3 The Forbidden Zone

The voltage range between $V_{IL(\max)}$ and $V_{IH(\min)}$ is the **undefined region**. In this zone:
- The gate may output either HIGH or LOW unpredictably.
- Both transistors in CMOS may conduct simultaneously → **shoot-through current** → power spike.
- The output transistor in TTL may operate in the active region → high power dissipation.

---

## 2.2 Noise Margins — Analytical Breakdown

For a guaranteed noise-immune system:

$$\boxed{NM_H = V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)}}$$

$$\boxed{NM_L = V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)}}$$

### 2.2.1 Worked Numerical Comparison (Standard V_CC = 5 V)

| Family | $V_{OH(\min)}$ | $V_{IH(\min)}$ | $NM_H$ | $V_{IL(\max)}$ | $V_{OL(\max)}$ | $NM_L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **74LS TTL** | 2.7 V | 2.0 V | **0.7 V** | 0.8 V | 0.5 V | **0.3 V** |
| **74HC CMOS** | 4.4 V | 3.5 V | **0.9 V** | 1.5 V | 0.33 V | **1.17 V** |
| **74HCT CMOS** | 4.4 V | 2.0 V | **2.4 V** | 0.8 V | 0.33 V | **0.47 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** HC CMOS has *asymmetric* margins favoring LOW; HCT is engineered to have *TTL-compatible* input thresholds while keeping CMOS-output drive strengths.

### 2.2.2 Worst-Case Design Philosophy

KTU expects you to use **worst-case values** in calculations, not typical values. The system must work when *every* component is at its *worst* simultaneously (statistical worst-case design). This is why we use $V_{OH(\min)}$ not $V_{OH(typ)}$.

---

## 2.3 Fan-Out — The Current Budget Model

### 2.3.1 The Mechanism

Every driven gate input acts as a **load**:
- In TTL: a LOW input sinks current from the driver (the multi-emitter input transistor is forward-biased).
- In CMOS: an input draws only leakage current (essentially zero) — but it adds **capacitance**.

### 2.3.2 Fan-Out Formula (Standard Method)

$$FO_{LOW} = \frac{I_{OL(\max)}}{I_{IL(\max)}}, \qquad FO_{HIGH} = \frac{I_{OH(\max)}}{I_{IH(\max)}}$$

$$\boxed{FO = \min(FO_{LOW}, \quad FO_{HIGH})}$$

### 2.3.3 Fan-Out in CMOS

Pure CMOS has a **DC fan-out of 50 or more** because DC input current is essentially zero. The **practical limitation** is *AC fan-out* — every added input adds capacitance, slowing down the transition.

$$f_{max,AC} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_{in} \cdot N}$$

where $N$ is the number of loads. **As N increases, maximum operating frequency drops.**

---

## 2.4 Propagation Delay — Physical Origin

### 2.4.1 RC Delay Model

Every gate output has an equivalent output resistance $R_{out}$ and every wire/input has a capacitance $C_L$. The result is a first-order RC low-pass:

$$\bar{t_p} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L$$

### 2.4.2 Why TTL is Faster (Historically)

In standard TTL, $R_{out}$ is small (the active pull-down transistor in saturation has low $R_{CE(sat)}$), so RC delay is small. **74LS** added Schottky diodes to prevent transistor saturation, eliminating stored charge and drastically speeding up turn-off.

### 2.4.3 Why Modern CMOS is Even Faster

Scaling down CMOS to smaller process nodes (180 nm → 65 nm → 14 nm) reduces both $R_{out}$ (shorter, wider channels) and $C_L$ (smaller gates), collapsing propagation delay to **picoseconds**.

---

## 2.5 Power Consumption — Two Regimes

### 2.5.1 Static Power

$$P_S = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CCL} \quad \text{(output LOW)}$$
$$P_S = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CCH} \quad \text{(output HIGH)}$$

For TTL: $P_S \approx 2\text{–}10$ mW per gate (non-zero due to the totem-pole output stage drawing current).

For CMOS: $P_S \approx V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} \approx \text{nW}$ per gate (only subthreshold leakage and reverse-biased diode leakage).

### 2.5.2 Dynamic Power (CMOS Dominated by This)

$$P_D = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = switching frequency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $V_{DD}^2$ dependence is the secret of low-power design.** Reducing supply voltage from 5 V to 3.3 V cuts dynamic power by 56%. This is why modern CPUs dynamically scale voltage.

### 2.5.3 Power-Delay Product (PDP) — The True Figure of Merit

$$\boxed{\text{PDP} = P_{avg} \times \bar{t_p} = \text{Energy per switching operation (Joules)}}$$

For a CMOS gate:
$$\text{PDP} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f \cdot \bar{t_p}$$

If $\bar{t_p} \propto C_L / V_{DD}$ (a reasonable approximation for short-channel devices), then:

$$\text{PDP} \propto C_L^2 \cdot V_{DD}$$

This means **lower capacitance AND lower voltage** both reduce the energy-per-operation, which is why modern chips use both small transistors *and* low voltages.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every row of this table. These are the seven formulas KTU 2024 examiners repeatedly test.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Noise Margin HIGH | $NM_H = V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Noise Margin LOW | $NM_L = V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Fan-out (LOW) | $FO_{LOW} = I_{OL(\max)} / I_{IL(\max)}$ | Unitless |
| 4 | Fan-out (HIGH) | $FO_{HIGH} = I_{OH(\max)} / I_{IH(\max)}$ | Unitless |
| 5 | Average Propagation Delay | $\bar{t_p} = (t_{pLH} + t_{pHL})/2$ | Seconds (s) |
| 6 | RC Delay Approximation | $\bar{t_p} \approx 0.69 R_{out} C_L$ | Seconds (s) |
| 7 | Dynamic Power (CMOS) | $P_D = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Power-Delay Product | $\text{PDP} = P_{avg} \times \bar{t_p}$ | Joules (J) |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility – Where These Numbers Matter

1. **PCB Design:** Noise margins determine how long a trace can run before picking up unacceptable interference. Higher NM = longer allowable trace length.
2. **Power Budgets:** A smartphone SoC dissipates ~5 W total across billions of gates — managed only through aggressive voltage/frequency scaling derived from the $CV^2f$ equation.
3. **Battery Life:** Choosing CMOS over TTL for a portable device can extend battery life by **100×** because static power drops from mW to nW.
4. **High-Speed Design:** In GHz processors, $t_p$ sets the **critical path delay** — the longest signal path that determines clock period.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Noise Margins from Specification Data

**Problem (typical KTU 3-mark setup):**
> A TTL gate has the following specifications at $V_{CC} = 5$ V: $V_{OH(\min)} = 2.7$ V, $V_{OL(\max)} = 0.4$ V, $V_{IH(\min)} = 2.0$ V, $V_{IL(\max)} = 0.8$ V. Calculate the noise margins.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.**
Noise margins are defined as the voltage guard-band between the driver's worst output and the receiver's worst input requirement. We must use *minimum* output voltages and *maximum* allowed input thresholds (worst-case design).

**Step 2 — Apply $NM_H$ formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)} \\
&= 2.7 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} \\
&= 0.7 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $NM_L$ formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_L &= V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)} \\
&= 0.8 \text{ V} - 0.4 \text{ V} \\
&= 0.4 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The gate can tolerate up to **0.7 V** of positive noise on a HIGH signal and **0.4 V** of negative noise on a LOW signal before logic corruption. The asymmetry reflects TTL's inherent HIGH-state weakness (output HIGH can drop toward 2.7 V).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Many students swap the subscripts — writing $V_{IH(\max)}$ instead of $V_{IH(\min)}$. Always use the *driver's worst output* (minimum for HIGH, maximum for LOW) against the *receiver's worst input* requirement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fan-Out with Current Budgeting

**Problem:**
> A 74LS00 NAND gate output can sink $I_{OL(\max)} = 8$ mA and source $I_{OH(\max)} = 0.4$ mA. Each driven 74LS00 input draws $I_{IL(\max)} = 0.4$ mA (in LOW state) and $I_{IH(\max)} = 20\ \mu$A (in HIGH state). Calculate the guaranteed fan-out.

**Step 1 — Identify DC current directions.**
In the LOW output state, the driver must **sink** all the current coming from inputs. In the HIGH output state, the driver must **source** current into the inputs.

**Step 2 — Calculate LOW-state fan-out.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FO_{LOW} &= \frac{I_{OL(\max)}}{I_{IL(\max)}} \\
&= \frac{8 \text{ mA}}{0.4 \text{ mA}} \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate HIGH-state fan-out.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FO_{HIGH} &= \frac{I_{OH(\max)}}{I_{IH(\max)}} \\
&= \frac{0.4 \text{ mA}}{20\ \mu\text{A}} \\
&= \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{400\ \mu\text{A}}{20\ \mu\text{A}} \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the worst case.**

$$
\boxed{FO = \min(20, 20) = 20 \text{ inputs}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Insight.**
The matched 20/20 ratio is coincidental here. In real-world 74LS, the HIGH-state source current is the bottleneck for most simple gates. Always report *both* sub-answers before taking the minimum — KTU gives partial marks for showing the work.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Propagation Delay from Measurement

**Problem:**
> A 74HC04 inverter is measured to have $t_{pLH} = 8$ ns and $t_{pHL} = 12$ ns when driving a 50 pF load. Find: (a) the average propagation delay, and (b) the propagation delay if the load is doubled.

**Step 1 — Average propagation delay.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t_p} &= \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} \\
&= \frac{8 + 12}{2} \text{ ns} \\
&= 10 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive the output resistance using RC model.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t_p} &\approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot C_L \\
10 \times 10^{-9} &\approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot 50 \times 10^{-12} \\
R_{out} &\approx \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{0.69 \times 50 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{34.5 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&\approx 290\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-calculate with doubled load (100 pF).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t_p}' &\approx 0.69 \cdot R_{out} \cdot (2 C_L) \\
&= 2 \cdot \bar{t_p} \\
&= 2 \cdot 10 \text{ ns} \\
&= 20 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Propagation delay scales **linearly** with load capacitance. This is why buffer trees are inserted in large ICs — to break large capacitances into smaller, parallel-driven pieces.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Power-Delay Product (Full Working)

**Problem:**
> A CMOS inverter operates at $V_{DD} = 5$ V, drives $C_L = 10$ pF, switches at $f = 100$ MHz with activity factor $\alpha = 0.5$. The average propagation delay is $\bar{t_p} = 5$ ns. Find: (a) dynamic power, (b) total power (assume static power = 0.1 mW), (c) PDP.

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f \\
&= 0.5 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (5)^2 \cdot (100 \times 10^6) \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 25 \cdot 100 \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 12.5 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tot} &= P_D + P_S \\
&= 12.5 \text{ mW} + 0.1 \text{ mW} \\
&= 12.6 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Power-Delay Product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDP} &= P_{tot} \cdot \bar{t_p} \\
&= (12.6 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9}) \\
&= 63 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} \\
&= 63 \text{ pJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**
Each switching event consumes **63 picojoules**. A billion-gate chip switching at 1 GHz would consume 63 kW — *clearly infeasible*. This is why real chips:
- Use lower $V_{DD}$ (1 V or less)
- Reduce activity factor via clock gating
- Use smaller $C_L$ through advanced process nodes

> [!NOTE]
> **The $V_{DD}^2$ Effect:** If we reduce $V_{DD}$ from 5 V to 1 V (5× lower), dynamic power drops by **25×**, making PDP = 2.5 pJ. This single equation explains the entire industry-wide voltage-scaling trend.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Characteristic | TTL (74LS) | CMOS (74HC) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static Power | 2 mW | ~0 W (leakage) | CMOS |
| Dynamic Power | Moderate | $CV^2f$ dominated | CMOS (at low f) |
| Propagation Delay | 5–10 ns | 8–15 ns | TTL (slightly) |
| Noise Margin (HIGH) | 0.7 V | 0.9 V | CMOS |
| Noise Margin (LOW) | 0.3 V | 1.17 V | CMOS |
| Fan-out (DC) | 20 | 50+ | CMOS |
| Power-Delay Product | ~50 pJ | ~10–30 pJ | CMOS |
| Supply Voltage Range | Fixed (5 V) | 2–6 V (wide) | CMOS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

This diagram shows how the four threshold voltages carve the supply range into allowed and forbidden regions.

**Reading the diagram:**
- Green nodes are valid thresholds; red is the forbidden zone.
- The arrows annotated as $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ are the *guard bands* (noise margins) you must compute.

```mermaid

    end

    end
```

```mermaid
    TotalP --> Dynamic
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: TTL NAND Gate (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputA["Input A"]
    InputB["Input B"]

    subgraph Q1["Multi-Emitter Input Transistor"]
        Stage1["Logic Level Translation\nand AND Function"]
    end

    subgraph Q2["Phase Splitter"]
        Stage2["Generates Complementary\nDrive Signals"]
    end

    subgraph Q3Q4["Output Stage Totem-Pole"]
        Stage3["Q3 Pull-Down NPN\nQ4 Pull-Up NPN"]
    end

    OutputY["Output Y = NOT A.B"]

    InputA --> Stage1
    InputB --> Stage1
    Stage1 --> Stage2
    Stage2 --> Stage3
    Stage3 --> OutputY

    style InputA fill:#17becf,color:#000
    style InputB fill:#17becf,color:#000

    style OutputY fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

**Engineering takeaway:** The totem-pole output stage (Q3/Q4) has both transistors stacked — when one is ON, the other is OFF. This minimizes static power consumption compared to a passive pull-up resistor, but creates transient current spikes during switching (which contribute to dynamic power).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define noise margin. List the typical noise margin values for standard TTL and CMOS logic families.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Noise Margin** is defined as the voltage difference between the guaranteed worst-case output voltage level of a logic gate and the guaranteed worst-case input voltage threshold required by the next gate. It quantifies the immunity of a logic family against unwanted electrical noise.

It has two forms:
- **Noise Margin HIGH:** $NM_H = V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)}$
- **Noise Margin LOW:** $NM_L = V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)}$

**Typical Values at $V_{CC} = 5$ V:**

| Family | $NM_H$ | $NM_L$ |
|---|---|---|
| Standard TTL (74LS) | ~0.7 V | ~0.3 V |
| CMOS (74HC) | ~0.9 V | ~1.17 V |

**Conclusion:** CMOS offers higher noise margins than TTL, making it more reliable in noisy environments. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: What is the Power-Delay Product (PDP) of a logic gate? Why is it called the Figure of Merit?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is defined as the product of the average power dissipation $P_{avg}$ of a logic gate and its average propagation delay $\bar{t_p}$.
>
> $$\text{PDP} = P_{avg} \times \bar{t_p} \quad \text{(unit: Joules)}$$
>
> It is called the **Figure of Merit** because it represents the **energy consumed by a gate per switching operation** — independent of operating frequency. A gate can be fast *or* low-power, but a good gate must have low PDP. Modern CMOS gates achieve PDPs in the femtojoule ($10^{-15}$ J) range, making them ideal for high-density, low-power integrated circuits. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. Always state **which family** you are analyzing before plugging in numbers.
> 2. Use **worst-case** values, *not* typical values — students routinely lose 2 marks for using $V_{OH(typ)}$ instead of $V_{OH(\min)}$.
> 3. Show **both** $FO_{LOW}$ and $FO_{HIGH}$ before taking the minimum.
> 4. Carry **units** throughout the calculation; final answer without units loses 1 mark.
> 5. In comparison questions, finish with a **summary table** — examiners award an easy 2 marks for a clean tabulation.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — (14 Marks)

**Q: Compare TTL and CMOS logic families with respect to the following electrical characteristics:**
**(a)** Logic levels and noise margins (7 marks)
**(b)** Fan-out, propagation delay, power consumption, and power-delay product (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Logic Levels & Noise Margins (7 marks)

**Logic Levels Table (at $V_{CC} = 5$ V):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TTL: } & V_{OH(\min)} = 2.7 \text{ V}, \quad V_{IH(\min)} = 2.0 \text{ V} \\
& V_{IL(\max)} = 0.8 \text{ V}, \quad V_{OL(\max)} = 0.5 \text{ V} \\
\text{CMOS: } & V_{OH(\min)} = 4.4 \text{ V}, \quad V_{IH(\min)} = 3.5 \text{ V} \\
& V_{IL(\max)} = 1.5 \text{ V}, \quad V_{OL(\max)} = 0.33 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Noise Margins Computation:**

For TTL:
$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= 2.7 - 2.0 = 0.7 \text{ V} \\
NM_L &= 0.8 - 0.5 = 0.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

For CMOS:
$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= 4.4 - 3.5 = 0.9 \text{ V} \\
NM_L &= 1.5 - 0.33 = 1.17 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Summary:**
- CMOS has wider HIGH and LOW validity regions.
- CMOS noise margins are more symmetric and consistently higher.
- TTL noise margins are asymmetric (HIGH is stronger than LOW).
- **Conclusion:** CMOS is more robust against noise.

#### Part (b) — Fan-out, Delay, Power & PDP (7 marks)

**Fan-out:**
- TTL fan-out is limited by DC current (typically 10–20).
- CMOS has very high DC fan-out (~50+) since CMOS inputs draw negligible DC current.
- CMOS fan-out is practically limited by AC loading (capacitance).

**Propagation Delay:**
- TTL: $\bar{t_p} \approx 5\text{–}10$ ns (74LS).
- CMOS (74HC): $\bar{t_p} \approx 8\text{–}15$ ns.
- Modern scaled CMOS is significantly faster than TTL.

**Power Consumption:**
- TTL static power: 2–10 mW per gate (significant even when idle).
- CMOS static power: ~nW per gate (only leakage).
- CMOS dynamic power: $P_D = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (dominates at high frequency).

**Power-Delay Product:**
- TTL PDP: ~50 pJ.
- CMOS PDP: ~10–30 pJ (much better).

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | TTL (74LS) | CMOS (74HC) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Noise Margin | 0.7 V / 0.3 V | 0.9 V / 1.17 V | CMOS |
| Fan-out | 10–20 | 50+ | CMOS |
| Delay | 5–10 ns | 8–15 ns | TTL |
| Static Power | 2–10 mW | ~nW | CMOS |
| PDP | ~50 pJ | ~10–30 pJ | CMOS |

**Conclusion:** CMOS is superior in nearly all modern design metrics. TTL retains niche uses in legacy systems and certain high-current drive applications. **(14 marks)**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**Q: A digital logic system uses a 74LS-series TTL gate driving multiple identical 74LS-series loads. The driver specifications are: $V_{OH(\min)} = 2.7$ V, $V_{OL(\max)} = 0.5$ V, $I_{OH(\max)} = 0.4$ mA, $I_{OL(\max)} = 8$ mA. The load specifications are: $V_{IH(\min)} = 2.0$ V, $V_{IL(\max)} = 0.8$ V, $I_{IH(\max)} = 20\ \mu$A, $I_{IL(\max)} = 0.4$ mA. The gate operates at $V_{CC} = 5$ V with measured $t_{pLH} = 6$ ns and $t_{pHL} = 10$ ns, and average static power of 4 mW.**

**(a)** Calculate the noise margins, fan-out, and average propagation delay. (7 marks)
**(b)** If the gate switches at a frequency of 25 MHz with activity factor $\alpha = 0.25$ and load capacitance $C_L = 20$ pF, calculate the total power consumption and the Power-Delay Product. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Noise Margins, Fan-out & Propagation Delay (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute noise margins.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NM_H &= V_{OH(\min)} - V_{IH(\min)} = 2.7 - 2.0 = 0.7 \text{ V} \\
NM_L &= V_{IL(\max)} - V_{OL(\max)} = 0.8 - 0.5 = 0.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute fan-out.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FO_{LOW} &= \frac{I_{OL(\max)}}{I_{IL(\max)}} = \frac{8 \text{ mA}}{0.4 \text{ mA}} = 20 \\
FO_{HIGH} &= \frac{I_{OH(\max)}}{I_{IH(\max)}} = \frac{0.4 \text{ mA}}{0.02 \text{ mA}} = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $FO = \min(20, 20) = \mathbf{20}$ loads.

**Step 3 — Compute average propagation delay.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t_p} &= \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = \frac{6 + 10}{2} = 8 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Power Consumption & PDP (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Dynamic power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{CC}^2 \cdot f \\
&= 0.25 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (5)^2 \cdot (25 \times 10^6) \\
&= 0.25 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 25 \cdot 25 \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.25 \cdot 12500 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 3125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} \\
&= 3.125 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{tot} &= P_S + P_D = 4 \text{ mW} + 3.125 \text{ mW} = 7.125 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power-Delay Product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDP} &= P_{tot} \times \bar{t_p} \\
&= (7.125 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-9}) \\
&= 57 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} \\
&= 57 \text{ pJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Noise Margins: $NM_H = 0.7$ V, $NM_L = 0.3$ V
- Fan-out: 20 loads
- Average Propagation Delay: 8 ns
- Total Power: 7.125 mW
- Power-Delay Product: 57 pJ

**(14 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin to Your Wall!**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Logic Levels** = four threshold voltages ($V_{IL}, V_{IH}, V_{OL}, V_{OH}$).
- ☐ **Noise Margins** = $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ (safety buffers against noise).
- ☐ **Fan-out** = max number of loads a driver can reliably supply (DC limit).
- ☐ **Propagation Delay** = time for signal to pass through gate (50% points).
- ☐ **Transition Time** = 10% to 90% rise/fall time.
- ☐ **Static Power** = power when output is not switching.
- ☐ **Dynamic Power** = $P_D = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (CMOS).
- ☐ **Power-Delay Product** = energy per switch = $P_{avg} \cdot \bar{t_p}$.

### TTL vs CMOS — Memorize the Winner for Each Parameter
- ☐ **Speed (delay):** TTL slightly faster historically, but modern CMOS wins.
- ☐ **Power (static):** CMOS *crushes* TTL (mW → nW).
- ☐ **Noise Margins:** CMOS wins (especially LOW state).
- ☐ **Fan-out (DC):** CMOS wins (50+ vs 10–20).
- ☐ **Supply Flexibility:** CMOS wins (2–6 V vs fixed 5 V for TTL).
- ☐ **Output Drive Strength at HIGH:** TTL often stronger (HC has weaker HIGH sourcing).

### Numerical Values to Memorize (at 5 V)
- ☐ TTL: $V_{OH(\min)} = 2.7$ V, $V_{OL(\max)} = 0.5$ V, $V_{IH(\min)} = 2.0$ V, $V_{IL(\max)} = 0.8$ V.
- ☐ CMOS 74HC: $V_{OH(\min)} = 4.4$ V, $V_{OL(\max)} = 0.33$ V, $V_{IH(\min)} = 3.5$ V, $V_{IL(\max)} = 1.5$ V.

### Common Pitfalls (Will Cost You Marks!)
- ☐ Don't use $V_{OH(typ)}$ — always use $V_{OH(\min)}$ (worst-case design).
- ☐ Don't forget to report BOTH $FO_{LOW}$ and $FO_{HIGH}$.
- ☐ Don't forget the unit "Joules" for PDP.
- ☐ Don't confuse *propagation delay* (50% to 50%) with *transition time* (10% to 90%).
- ☐ In CMOS dynamic power, the activity factor $\alpha$ is often missing in textbook solutions — include it!

### Engineering Mantra
> *"A good gate is fast, frugal, and friendly — low power, low delay, and high fan-out. PDP captures all three in one number."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TTL Inverter - Circuit Description and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIT205 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN (ELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** TTL Inverter - Circuit Description and Operation # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) Inverter** is the fundamental NOT-gate building block of the bipolar junction transistor (BJT) based digital logic family, in which the input–output logic inversion is performed entirely by interconnected bipolar transistors (no resistors or diodes performing the logic decision). The basic TTL gate is built around a **multi-emitter input transistor (Q1)**, a **phase-splitter transistor (Q2)**, and a **totem-pole (push-pull) output stage** formed by Q3 and Q4. Tying all emitters of Q1 to a single node converts the canonical **TTL NAND gate** into a TTL **inverter** (NOT gate), because $\overline{A \cdot A} = \overline{A}$. The standard TTL inverter operates from a single **VCC = +5 V** supply and is fully compatible with the **74xx series** commercial TTL sub-families (e.g., **74LS00, 74LS04, 74S00, 74AS00, 74F00**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture — "The Three-Story Building with a Single Door"** Imagine a 3-story building that delivers mail to the ground floor: - **Ground floor (Q1 — the multi-emitter input):** This is the *mailroom* with many letter slots. If ANY slot receives a LOW signal, current drains out of the building through that slot and the entire mail system is "killed." Only when ALL slots are HIGH does the current flow *upward* to the upper floors. - **Middle floor (Q2 — the phase splitter):** This is the *switchboard operator*. When mail arrives from below, the operator closes one circuit (top floor) and opens another (bottom floor) — always in *opposite* directions. This is the phase inversion. - **Top floor (Q3) and basement (Q4) — the totem-pole:** These are two *elevators* working in opposition. When the top one is up, the basement one is down, and vice versa. The output is whatever the top elevator delivers (HIGH) or the basement delivers (LOW), but never both at once. So the input mail "decision" cascades upward, the operator inverts it, and the dual elevator delivers a clean, amplified, opposite signal to the output — a textbook **NOT operation**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard TTL Supply & Logic Levels (74xx series, VCC = +5 V):** > - **VIL(max) = 0.8 V** — maximum input voltage recognized as LOW > - **VIH(min) = 2.0 V** — minimum input voltage recognized as HIGH > - **VOL(max) = 0.4 V** — maximum output in LOW state > - **VOH(min) = 2.4 V** — minimum output in HIGH state > - **Typical propagation delay t_pd ≈ 10 ns** (standard TTL); 7404 hex-inverter > - **Static power dissipation P_D ≈ 10 mW per gate** (standard TTL) > - **Noise margin: NM_H = NM_L ≈ 0.4 V** > - **Fan-out ≈ 10** (can drive 10 standard TTL loads) > - **Base-emitter voltage drop V_BE(on) ≈ 0.7 V** > - **Base-collector voltage drop V_BC(on) ≈ 0.7 V** > - **Saturation voltage V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology of the Basic TTL Inverter The basic TTL inverter (derived from the standard TTL NAND by tying all inputs together) consists of the following sub-blocks: | Sub-block | Component | Function | |-----------|-----------|----------| | **Input stage** | Q1 (multi-emitter NPN) | Implements the AND/OR logic of inputs | | **Base bias** | R1 (≈ 4 kΩ) | Pulls Q1 base to VCC, sets base current | | **Phase splitter** | Q2 (NPN) | Splits the signal into in-phase (emitter) and out-of-phase (collector) drives | | **Phase-splitter load** | R2 (≈ 1.6 kΩ) | Collector pull-up of Q2 | | **Phase-splitter emitter resistor** | R3 (≈ 1 kΩ) | Emitter pull-down of Q2 | | **Pull-up transistor** | Q3 (NPN, Darlington in some variants) | Sources current to the load (output HIGH) | | **Pull-down transistor** | Q4 (NPN) | Sinks current from the load (output LOW) | | **Output pull-up resistor** | R4 (≈ 130 Ω) | Limits shoot-through current, holds Q3 OFF in LOW state | | **Input clamp diodes** | D1, D2 (sometimes D3) | Suppress negative input voltage transients (ringing) | > [!NOTE] > **Why a multi-emitter transistor?** In early DTL (Diode-Transistor Logic) families, the AND function was implemented using multiple input diodes. Replacing these diodes with multiple emitter junctions of a single transistor (Q1) reduces device count, lowers parasitic capacitance, and dramatically increases switching speed — this is the core innovation that defines TTL. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Two-State Analysis) ### Case A — Input LOW (V_in = 0.2 V, Logic 0) 1. The base–emitter junction of Q1 corresponding to the LOW input becomes **forward biased** (V_BE1 ≈ 0.7 V). Current flows from VCC → R1 → Q1(B-E) → input terminal → ground. 2. Q1 enters **saturation**; its collector voltage drops to roughly V_in + V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V + 0.2 V = 0.4 V. 3. The voltage at Q2's base is approximately 0.4 V, which is insufficient to forward-bias the Q2 base–emitter junction (would require ≥ 0.7 V). Therefore **Q2 is OFF (cutoff)**. 4. With Q2 OFF, its collector is pulled HIGH through R2; this high voltage drives Q3 into **saturation** through the base–emitter path of Q3 (and the level-shift diodes if present). 5. Q2's emitter sits near 0 V (no emitter current), so the base of Q4 has no drive — **Q4 is OFF (cutoff)**. 6. The output is connected to VCC through R4 and the saturated Q3 → **V_out ≈ VOH ≈ 3.4 V** (HIGH level, after subtracting 2 V_BE drops in the Darlington path of typical TTL). ### Case B — Input HIGH (V_in = 5.0 V, Logic 1) 1. All base–emitter junctions of Q1 are **reverse biased** (V_in ≥ V_B1 ≈ 2.1 V, so the base is at most 2.1 V, while emitters are at 5.0 V — junction reverse biased). 2. The Q1 **base–collector** junction becomes **forward biased**. Current flows from VCC → R1 → Q1(B-C) → Q2(B-E) → R3 → ground. 3. Q2 receives substantial base current → Q2 enters **saturation**. 4. Q2's collector voltage drops to V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V. This is too low to keep Q3 ON (Q3 base would need ≥ 0.7 V), so **Q3 is OFF**. 5. Q2's emitter rises to ≈ 0.7 V (because Q2 is saturated and Q4's base–emitter is forward biased), which drives **Q4 into saturation**. 6. The output is pulled to Q4's saturation voltage through the conducting Q4 → **V_out ≈ VOL ≈ 0.2 V** (LOW level). ## 2.3 The Totem-Pole Output Stage — Key Engineering Insight > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is the output stage called "totem-pole"?** Because Q3 (top) and Q4 (bottom) are stacked in series between VCC and GND, like the figures carved one on top of another on a Native American totem pole. They NEVER conduct simultaneously (this would cause a destructive VCC-to-GND short circuit known as **"shoot-through"** or **"crowbar current"**). The small resistor R4 between them limits any brief overlap current during switching transitions. **Advantages of the totem-pole output:** - Low output impedance in BOTH states (active pull-up + active pull-down) → fast rise/fall times. - Symmetrical drive capability (source current in HIGH, sink current in LOW). - Low static power dissipation (no DC path from VCC to GND in steady state). - No external pull-up resistor required (unlike open-collector TTL). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU university exam questions.** | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Value | |---|--------------------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $V_{OH} = V_{CC} - V_{BE3} - V_{BE(Darlington)}$ | Output HIGH level (Q3 saturated path) | ≈ **3.4 V** for VCC = 5 V | | 2 | $V_{OL} = V_{CE4(sat)}$ | Output LOW level (Q4 saturated) | ≈ **0.2 V** | | 3 | $V_{IL(max)} = 0.8$ V | Max input recognized as LOW | **0.8 V** | | 4 | $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0$ V | Min input recognized as HIGH | **2.0 V** | | 5 | $NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}$ | HIGH-state noise margin | **0.4 V** | | 6 | $NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}$ | LOW-state noise margin | **0.4 V** | | 7 | $Fan\text{-}out = \dfrac{I_{OL(max)}}{I_{IL(max)}}$ | Maximum driven loads | **10** (std TTL) | | 8 | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | ≈ **10 ns** | | 9 | $P_D = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC(avg)}$ | Static power dissipation | ≈ **10 mW** | | 10 | $PDP = P_D \cdot t_{pd}$ | Power-delay product (figure of merit) | ≈ **100 pJ** | | 11 | $I_{B1} \approx \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE1} - V_{OL}}{R_1}$ | Base current of Q1 in LOW-input state | ≈ **1 mA** | | 12 | $I_{B2} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BC1} - V_{BE2} - I_{E2} R_3}{R_1}$ | Base current of Q2 in HIGH-input state | ≈ **0.5 mA** | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility TTL inverters (and the larger TTL family) dominated digital design from the 1960s through the late 1980s. Even today, TTL-compatible logic levels are still used as the **industry-standard "5-V logic" interface** in: - Legacy industrial control systems (PLCs with 24 V/5 V interface cards) - Microcontroller GPIO (older 8051, PIC, HC11, AVR families all accept TTL thresholds) - Test equipment (logic analyzers, oscilloscopes with TTL trigger inputs) - Glue logic interfacing between modern 3.3 V CMOS and legacy 5 V systems - Educational laboratory trainer boards used in Indian engineering colleges (the classic "Digital Lab Kit" with 7404, 7400, 7476, 7490 ICs) # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output HIGH Voltage (V_OH) The output HIGH path is: VCC → R4 → Q3 (saturated, Darlington pair in 74xx) → output node. Treating the Darlington as two stacked V_BE drops, plus the saturated collector–emitter drop of the upper transistor, we can write: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{OH} &= V_{CC} - V_{BE(Q3a)} - V_{BE(Q3b)} - V_{CE(Q3a,sat)} \\ &\approx V_{CC} - 2V_{BE} - V_{CE(sat)} \\ &\approx 5.0 - 2(0.7) - 0.2 \\ &\approx 3.4 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic of the conversion:** - Line 1: Trace the voltage drops along the conducting path from VCC to V_out. - Line 2: Substitute standard BJT V_BE(on) ≈ 0.7 V and V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V. - Line 3: Numerical evaluation gives the KTU-stated typical V_OH of 3.4 V. > [!NOTE] > For the **simple 3-transistor TTL** (without Darlington output, used in early textbook derivations), V_OH ≈ V_CC − V_BE = 4.3 V. The 74LS and 74AS families use Darlington / Schottky-clamped Q3 to keep V_OH > 2.7 V even under full loading. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output LOW Voltage (V_OL) In the LOW state, Q4 is saturated and Q3 is OFF. The output is taken at Q4's collector: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{OL} &= V_{CE4(sat)} \\ &\approx 0.2 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** Q4 is driven into hard saturation by the emitter current of the phase splitter Q2. Hence the output node sits at the saturation voltage of Q4 above ground. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Base Current of Q2 in HIGH-Input State (i_B2) When V_in = HIGH (5.0 V), the Q1 base is held at: $$ V_{B1} = V_{in} + V_{BE1(rev)} \quad \text{(reverse-biased B-E, so } V_{B1} = V_{in} \text{ approximately)} $$ But more accurately, the Q1 base sits at: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{B1} &= V_{BC1} + V_{BE2} + V_{E2} \\ &= V_{BC1} + V_{BE2} + I_{E2} R_3 \end{aligned} $$ Applying KVL around the loop VCC → R1 → Q1(B-C) → Q2(B-E) → R3 → GND: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{CC} &= I_{B1} R_1 + V_{BC1(on)} + V_{BE2(on)} + I_{E2} R_3 \\ I_{B1} &= \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BC1} - V_{BE2} - I_{E2} R_3}{R_1} \\ &\approx \dfrac{5.0 - 0.7 - 0.7 - (1.6)(1.0)}{4.0 \, \text{k}\Omega} \\ &\approx \dfrac{5.0 - 0.7 - 0.7 - 1.6}{4 \, \text{k}\Omega} \\ &\approx \dfrac{2.0}{4 \, \text{k}\Omega} \\ &\approx 0.5 \, \text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic of each step:** - **Line 1:** KVL — sum of drops across R1, the Q1 B-C junction, Q2 B-E junction, and R3 equals VCC. - **Line 2:** Algebraic isolation of I_B1. - **Line 3:** Substitute typical values (V_CC = 5 V, V_BC1 = V_BE2 = 0.7 V, I_E2 R_3 = 1.6 V using R3 = 1 kΩ, R_1 = 4 kΩ). - **Line 4:** Numerical evaluation of numerator. - **Line 5:** Final result of 0.5 mA of base drive into Q2. This 0.5 mA is more than enough to drive Q2 into deep saturation, since Q2's β_typical ≈ 50 means it only needs ≈ 0.04 mA to saturate. The excess base current is sunk by Q2's collector node into R2. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Verification of Q2 Cutoff in LOW-Input State When V_in = LOW (0.2 V), Q1 is in saturation. The voltage at Q2's base is: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{B2} &= V_{in} + V_{CE1(sat)} \\ &= 0.2 + 0.2 \\ &= 0.4 \, \text{V} \end{aligned} $$ For Q2 to turn ON, we require V_BE2 ≥ 0.7 V. Since 0.4 V < 0.7 V, **Q2 is firmly in cutoff** ✓. This confirms that a LOW input reliably keeps Q2 OFF and forces the output HIGH. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — TTL Inverter States | State Parameter | V_in = LOW (0.2 V) | V_in = HIGH (5.0 V) | |-----------------|--------------------|--------------------| | Q1 | Saturated (B-E forward) | Reverse-biased B-E, forward B-C | | Q2 | Cutoff (V_BE2 = 0.4 V < 0.7 V) | Saturated (I_B2 = 0.5 mA) | | Q3 | ON (Darlington saturated) | OFF (V_B3 too low) | | Q4 | OFF | Saturated | | V_out | HIGH (≈ 3.4 V) | LOW (≈ 0.2 V) | | Logic function | $\overline{0} = 1$ ✓ | $\overline{1} = 0$ ✓ | | Current path | VCC → R4 → Q3 → output → load | Output → Q4 → GND (sinking) | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the TTL Inverter The diagram below maps the **signal flow** between the four functional sub-blocks of the TTL inverter (input stage, phase splitter, and the totem-pole output pair). This block view is preferred over a literal Mermaid transistor schematic because Mermaid cannot render the multi-emitter NPN transistor faithfully; the block view captures all causal interactions in the KTU-syllabus scope. ```mermaid graph TB Vin["Input Voltage V_in
0.2 V to 5.0 V"] VCC["VCC Supply
+5.0 V DC"] GND["Ground Reference
0.0 V"] R1["R1 Base Resistor
approximately 4 kOhm"] Q1["Q1 Multi-Emitter NPN
Input AND Logic Stage"] Q2["Q2 NPN Phase Splitter
Inverts and Splits Signal"] R2["R2 Collector Resistor
approximately 1.6 kOhm"] R3["R3 Emitter Resistor
approximately 1 kOhm"] Q3["Q3 Upper Output NPN
Pull Up Device Totem Pole"] R4["R4 Pull Up Resistor
approximately 130 Ohm"] Q4["Q4 Lower Output NPN
Pull Down Device Totem Pole"] Vout["Output Voltage V_out
Inverted Logic Level"] VCC --- R1 R1 --- Q1 Vin --- Q1 Q1 --- Q2 VCC --- R2 R2 --- Q2 Q2 --- R3 R3 --- GND Q2 --- Q3 VCC --- R4 R4 --- Q3 Q3 --- Vout Q2 --- Q4 Q4 --- GND Q4 --- Vout ``` ## 4.2 Signal-Conditioning State Diagram The diagram below captures the **two causal states** of the TTL inverter and how each transistor sub-block responds. This is a powerful KTU exam-style flowchart that examiners often reward as evidence of conceptual clarity. ```mermaid graph TB S1["State 1 Input V_in equals 0.2 V LOW"] S2["State 2 Input V_in equals 5.0 V HIGH"] A1["Q1 Base Emitter Forward Biased
Q1 Saturates"] A2["Q1 Base Emitter Reverse Biased
Q1 Base Collector Forward Biased"] B1["Q2 Base Voltage equals 0.4 V
Q2 Remains in Cutoff"] B2["Q2 Base Current equals 0.5 mA
Q2 Enters Saturation"] C1["Q3 Pull Up Conducts via R2
Q4 Remains OFF"] C2["Q3 Pull Up Remains OFF
Q4 Pull Down Saturates"] D1["V_out equals VOH approximately 3.4 V Logic 1"] D2["V_out equals VOL approximately 0.2 V Logic 0"] S1 --> A1 S1 --> B1 S1 --> C1 S1 --> D1 S2 --> A2 S2 --> B2 S2 --> C2 S2 --> D2 ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Reference Schematic (Textbook Standard) For quick desk-drawing in your KTU answer script, the standard schematic is laid out as follows (use this as a reference template to draw during the exam): ``` VCC (+5 V) | R1 (4 kΩ) | +-----+-----+-----+ (Multiple emitters of Q1; tie all to | | | one node to form an INVERTER) B [E1] [E2] [E3] ← INPUT NODE | | | | Q1 | | | (Multi-emitter NPN, Q1) | | | | C | | | | +-----+-----+ | | +----------+---→ Base of Q2 | (Q1 collector drives Q2 base) VCC | R2 (1.6 kΩ) | +--------→ Collector of Q2 ----→ Base of Q3 | | | B (Q3 = upper totem-pole NPN) | | | C | | | E ----→ V_out (output node) | | | +----+ | R4 (130 Ω) | | | C | | (Q4 = lower totem-pole NPN) | B ←--- Emitter of Q2 (via Q2) | | | E | | | GND | +--------→ Emitter of Q2 → R3 (1 kΩ) → GND ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners award full marks only when you (a) draw the multi-emitter Q1 with all emitters tied together for an inverter, (b) clearly label R1, R2, R3, R4 with approximate values, and (c) show the totem-pole output (Q3 over Q4 with R4 between them) rather than a single pull-down transistor. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"What is a multi-emitter transistor? Justify its use as the input device in a basic TTL gate."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A multi-emitter transistor is an NPN bipolar junction transistor fabricated with **multiple (typically 2, 3, or 4) emitter regions** sharing a common base and a common collector on a single silicon die. It is used as the input device in TTL because it inherently performs the **logical AND function** on its multiple input emitters: a LOW on any emitter forward-biases the corresponding base–emitter junction and diverts the base current away from the collector, forcing the output transistor OFF. Only when **all** emitters are HIGH does the base current flow into the collector to drive the next stage. Compared to the DTL predecessor (which used multiple input diodes), the multi-emitter transistor **reduces the silicon area, lowers input capacitance, and increases switching speed**, which is the central innovation of the TTL family. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Why is the TTL output stage called a 'totem-pole' configuration? State two of its advantages."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The TTL output stage is called a **totem-pole** because two transistors (Q3 — the upper pull-up, and Q4 — the lower pull-down) are stacked in series between VCC and GND, resembling the figures stacked one on top of the other on a traditional totem pole. The two transistors are driven in anti-phase by the phase-splitter Q2, so only one conducts at a time. **Two advantages:** 1. **Low output impedance in both states** — active pull-up and active pull-down ensure fast rise and fall times, enabling higher operating frequencies. 2. **Symmetrical drive capability** — the gate can source current to a load in the HIGH state and sink current from a load in the LOW state with comparable strength, eliminating the need for an external pull-up resistor (as required in open-collector TTL). **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024] **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **operation of the basic TTL inverter** in detail. Discuss the conduction states of Q1, Q2, Q3 and Q4 for both input conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **expression for V_OH and V_OL** of a basic TTL inverter. Compute their typical values when VCC = 5 V. Comment on the **noise margins** obtained. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):** The basic TTL inverter is obtained by tying all the emitters of the multi-emitter input transistor Q1 to a single input node. The circuit has three functional stages: - **Input stage:** Q1 (multi-emitter NPN) with base resistor R1 (≈ 4 kΩ) tied to VCC. - **Phase-splitter stage:** Q2 (NPN) with collector resistor R2 (≈ 1.6 kΩ) and emitter resistor R3 (≈ 1 kΩ). - **Totem-pole output stage:** Q3 (upper NPN, sometimes Darlington) and Q4 (lower NPN) with R4 (≈ 130 Ω) between them. **Step 2 — Operation for V_in = LOW (3 marks):** - Q1's base–emitter junction becomes forward biased; Q1 enters **saturation**. - Voltage at Q2's base = V_in + V_CE1(sat) = 0.2 + 0.2 = **0.4 V**, insufficient to forward-bias Q2 (needs ≥ 0.7 V). So **Q2 is OFF**. - Q2's collector is pulled HIGH through R2 → Q3 turns **ON** (saturated, Darlington in some variants). - Q2's emitter is near 0 V → no base drive to Q4 → **Q4 is OFF**. - Output node is connected through Q3 and R4 to VCC → **V_out = HIGH (≈ 3.4 V)**. **Step 3 — Operation for V_in = HIGH (2 marks):** - Q1's B-E junctions are reverse biased; the B-C junction is forward biased. - Current flows VCC → R1 → Q1(B-C) → Q2(B-E) → R3 → GND; Q2 receives I_B2 ≈ 0.5 mA → **Q2 saturates**. - Q2's collector drops to V_CE2(sat) ≈ 0.2 V → Q3 is **OFF** (insufficient base drive). - Q2's emitter rises and drives Q4 into **saturation**. - Output node is pulled to Q4's saturation voltage → **V_out = LOW (≈ 0.2 V)**. The circuit therefore realizes the **NOT function** (inverter). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Derivation of V_OH (3 marks):** The HIGH-output path is VCC → R4 → Q3 (Darlington saturated) → output. Hence: $$ V_{OH} = V_{CC} - 2V_{BE} - V_{CE3(sat)} = 5.0 - 2(0.7) - 0.2 = 3.4 \text{ V} $$ (For a simple 3-transistor TTL without Darlington output, V_OH = V_CC − V_BE = 4.3 V.) **Derivation of V_OL (2 marks):** The LOW-output node sits at the saturation collector–emitter voltage of Q4: $$ V_{OL} = V_{CE4(sat)} \approx 0.2 \text{ V} $$ **Noise Margin Calculation (2 marks):** $$ NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)} = 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.4 \text{ V} $$ $$ NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)} = 0.8 - 0.4 = 0.4 \text{ V} $$ **Conclusion:** Both noise margins are equal at **0.4 V**, giving a total noise immunity of **0.8 V** — modest by modern standards but adequate for well-designed 5 V digital systems in laboratory conditions. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 — Alternative] **(a)** Draw the **circuit diagram of a 3-input TTL NAND gate** and explain its operation for all eight input combinations. Show that the gate reduces to an **inverter** when all three inputs are tied together. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With relevant expressions, explain the **concepts of fan-out, propagation delay, and power-delay product** for a TTL inverter. Compare standard TTL with **Schottky TTL (74S/74LS)** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Circuit (1 mark):** Same topology as the TTL inverter, but Q1 is fabricated with **three emitters** (E1, E2, E3) instead of one. The collectors of all three emitters are common (tied to the base of Q2). Each emitter is an independent input. **Logic function realised (1 mark):** With multi-emitter Q1 performing AND of the inputs and Q2/Q3/Q4 performing inversion, the output is: $$ F = \overline{A \cdot B \cdot C} $$ **Operation table for all 8 input combinations (4 marks):** | A | B | C | Q1 state | Q2 state | Q4 state | Output F | |---|---|---|----------|----------|----------|----------| | 0 | 0 | 0 | Sat (B-E fwd on all 3) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 0 | 0 | 1 | Sat (B-E fwd on A,B) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 0 | 1 | 0 | Sat (B-E fwd on A,C) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 0 | 1 | 1 | Sat (B-E fwd on A) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 1 | 0 | 0 | Sat (B-E fwd on B,C) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 1 | 0 | 1 | Sat (B-E fwd on B) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 1 | 1 | 0 | Sat (B-E fwd on C) | OFF | OFF | 1 | | 1 | 1 | 1 | All B-E rev; B-C fwd | SAT | SAT | 0 | **Reduction to an inverter (1 mark):** When A = B = C (all three emitters tied to a single node X), the gate becomes: $$ F = \overline{X \cdot X \cdot X} = \overline{X} \quad \text{(NOT/inverter function)} $$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Fan-out (2 marks):** Fan-out is the maximum number of standard TTL loads that the output of a TTL gate can reliably drive. Mathematically: $$ \text{Fan-out} = \frac{I_{OL(max)}}{I_{IL(max)}} = \frac{16 \, \text{mA}}{1.6 \, \text{mA}} = 10 $$ It is **10** for standard 74xx TTL. Exceeding this degrades V_OL and may cause the gate to leave its specified logic level. **Propagation Delay (2 marks):** Propagation delay is the time between the 50 % transition point of the input and the corresponding 50 % transition of the output. Two components exist: $t_{pLH}$ (LOW-to-HIGH) and $t_{pHL}$ (HIGH-to-LOW). The average is: $$ t_{pd} = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} \approx 10 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(standard TTL)} $$ **Power-Delay Product (1 mark):** $$ PDP = P_D \cdot t_{pd} \approx 10 \text{ mW} \times 10 \text{ ns} = 100 \text{ pJ} $$ This is the **figure of merit** of a logic family — lower PDP means more efficient gates. **Comparison Table — Standard TTL vs Schottky TTL (2 marks):** | Parameter | Standard TTL (74xx) | Schottky TTL (74S/74LS) | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------------| | $t_{pd}$ (speed) | ≈ 10 ns | ≈ 3 ns (74S) / 9.5 ns (74LS) | | $P_D$ per gate | ≈ 10 mW | ≈ 19 mW (74S) / 2 mW (74LS) | | Fan-out | 10 | 20 (74LS) | | Key innovation | Multi-emitter Q1 | **Schottky-clamped** transistors prevent saturation → faster turn-off | | Use today | Educational labs | Legacy high-speed systems | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places KTU students LOSE marks on TTL Inverter questions — read this carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting to tie the multi-emitter inputs together** when describing an inverter. If you draw a 3-emitter Q1 with three separate input terminals, you are describing a NAND, NOT an inverter. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this ambiguity. > 2. **Confusing Q1's base–emitter and base–collector junctions.** In the HIGH-input state, it is the **B-C** junction that conducts (forward biased), not the B-E. Many students write "Q1 conducts from base to emitter" — this is wrong. Mark loss: **1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting the level-shift diodes / clamp diodes D1, D2** in the schematic. While not always drawn in simplified textbook diagrams, KTU 2024 scheme examiners expect them, especially in 7-mark detailed questions. Deduction: **1 mark**. > 4. **Not labelling R1, R2, R3, R4 with approximate values.** A "block diagram only" answer without component values loses **1–2 marks** depending on the question's CO mapping. > 5. **Omitting the noise-margin numerical calculation.** A derivation-only answer without plugging in the values (NM_H = NM_L = 0.4 V) is considered incomplete by KTU's ESE marking scheme. > 6. **Writing V_OH = V_CC directly.** This is a frequent slip. The correct expression is V_OH = V_CC − 2V_BE − V_CE(sat) for the Darlington output stage. Always show the V_BE drops. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - [x] **TTL = Transistor-Transistor Logic**, VCC = **+5 V** standard, current-sinking logic family. - [x] **Three functional stages:** Input (Q1 multi-emitter) → Phase splitter (Q2) → Totem-pole output (Q3 + Q4). - [x] **Multi-emitter Q1** performs AND of inputs; ties all emitters together → converts NAND into **inverter**. - [x] **Input LOW → Q1 saturates → Q2 OFF → Q3 ON → Q4 OFF → V_out = HIGH (≈ 3.4 V).** - [x] **Input HIGH → Q1 B-C forward → Q2 saturates → Q3 OFF → Q4 ON → V_out = LOW (≈ 0.2 V).** - [x] **V_OH = V_CC − 2V_BE − V_CE(sat) ≈ 3.4 V ; V_OL = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V.** - [x] **Noise margins:** NM_H = NM_L = **0.4 V** (total noise immunity 0.8 V). - [x] **Standard TTL parameters:** $t_{pd}$ ≈ 10 ns, $P_D$ ≈ 10 mW, fan-out = 10, PDP ≈ 100 pJ. - [x] **Totem-pole output** = Q3 (top) + Q4 (bottom); never conduct simultaneously → no VCC-to-GND short. - [x] **Shoot-through / crowbar current:** brief current spike during switching; limited by R4. - [x] **D1, D2 are input clamp diodes** — protect against negative voltage transients on the input line. - [x] **Inverter logic identity:** $\overline{A \cdot A} = \overline{A}$ — all inputs tied to a single node realise the NOT function. - [x] **Schottky TTL** (74S/74LS) uses **Schottky-clamped transistors** to prevent saturation, achieving $t_{pd} \approx 3$ ns. - [x] **Always show component values** (R1 ≈ 4 kΩ, R2 ≈ 1.6 kΩ, R3 ≈ 1 kΩ, R4 ≈ 130 Ω) in your circuit diagram for full KTU marks. - [x] **Fan-out formula:** $\text{Fan-out} = I_{OL(max)} / I_{IL(max)}$. - [x] **Propagation delay formula:** $t_{pd} = (t_{pLH} + t_{pHL})/2$. - [x] **Power-delay product:** $PDP = P_D \cdot t_{pd}$ — universal figure of merit for digital logic families.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIT205 - LOGIC CIRCUIT DESIGN (ELECTRONICS AND INSTRUMENTATION)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CMOS Inverter - Circuit Description and Operation, CMOS NAND, CMOS NOR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is CMOS Technology?

**CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** is a VLSI fabrication technology that uses complementary pairs of **p-channel (PMOS)** and **n-channel (NMOS)** MOSFETs to implement digital logic functions. The word *Complementary* refers to the fact that for every logic gate, a PMOS transistor is paired with an NMOS transistor such that exactly one of them is ON when the other is OFF in steady state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEIT205 / Module 4):**
> The student must clearly understand the construction, working principle, and analytical behavior of the **CMOS Inverter**, **2-input CMOS NAND**, and **2-input CMOS NOR** gates, including the concepts of pull-up network (PUN) and pull-down network (PDN).

## 1.2 The CMOS Inverter — Formal Definition

The **CMOS Inverter** (also called a **NOT gate**) is the fundamental building block of all CMOS digital circuits. It consists of:
- **One PMOS transistor** as the **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** connected between **V_DD** and the output node.
- **One NMOS transistor** as the **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** connected between the output node and **GND (V_SS)**.
- The gates of both transistors are tied together and form the **input (V_in)**.
- The drains of both transistors are tied together and form the **output (V_out)**.
- The source of the PMOS is at **V_DD**, and the source of the NMOS is at **GND**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** In steady state, the CMOS inverter has **no direct conducting path between V_DD and GND**, which results in **near-zero static power dissipation**. This is the single most important advantage of CMOS over TTL logic families.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A "Water-Pipe Switch"

Imagine two water pipes connected vertically:
- The **upper pipe** (PMOS) draws water from a tank (V_DD) and is normally **closed** (OFF).
- The **lower pipe** (NMOS) drains water to the ground (GND) and is normally **closed** (OFF).
- A single lever (the input voltage) controls both pipes **inversely**:
  - Push the lever down → upper pipe **opens** (water flows down) and lower pipe **closes** → output is **HIGH (1)**.
  - Pull the lever up → upper pipe **closes** and lower pipe **opens** (water drains) → output is **LOW (0)**.

This **complementary (push-pull)** action is exactly what happens in the CMOS inverter. Crucially, the two pipes are **never open at the same time**, so water (current) never flows continuously from tank to drain — analogous to **zero static current** in CMOS.

## 1.4 CMOS NAND and NOR Gates — Formal Definitions

A **CMOS NAND gate** uses:
- **Two PMOS transistors in parallel** in the PUN.
- **Two NMOS transistors in series** in the PDN.

A **CMOS NOR gate** uses:
- **Two PMOS transistors in series** in the PUN.
- **Two NMOS transistors in parallel** in the PDN.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Exams:**
> - **NAND** → PMOS are in **PARALLEL** (P-Parallel, think *"NAND = Not AND, so PUN = AND structure in parallel"*) and NMOS are in **SERIES**.
> - **NOR** → PMOS are in **SERIES** and NMOS are in **PARALLEL** (mirror image of NAND).
> - **Rule of Thumb:** The PUN and PDN are **dual networks** — series becomes parallel, parallel becomes series.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are standard technology parameters used in KTU-level CMOS analysis:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supply voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **3.3 V or 5 V** | Volts |
| NMOS threshold voltage | $V_{Tn}$ | **0.5 to 1.0 V** | Volts |
| PMOS threshold voltage | $V_{Tp}$ | **−0.5 to −1.0 V** | Volts |
| Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox}$ | **~10⁻⁸** | F/cm² |
| Switching threshold | $V_M$ | $\approx V_{DD}/2$ | Volts |
| Logic levels | $V_{OH}, V_{OL}$ | $\approx V_{DD}, \approx 0$ | Volts |
