# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS Inverter — Detailed Operational Analysis

The CMOS inverter's operation can be divided into **three regions** of the input voltage $V_{in}$, and the corresponding **five operating regions** of the transistors.

### Region 1: $0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$
- **NMOS:** OFF (cutoff), because $V_{GS} = V_{in} < V_{Tn}$.
- **PMOS:** ON (linear/triode region), because $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{in} > |V_{Tp}|$.
- **Output:** Pulled up to $V_{DD}$ through the conducting PMOS. Hence $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.

### Region 2: $V_{Tn} < V_{in} < (V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|)$
- **NMOS:** ON, operating in **saturation** region.
- **PMOS:** ON, operating in **saturation** region.
- **Output:** A high current flows momentarily from $V_{DD}$ to GND, but the output voltage is determined by the balance of the two transistors. Both are in saturation, and $V_{out}$ decreases sharply as $V_{in}$ increases.

### Region 3: $(V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|) < V_{in} \le V_{DD}$
- **NMOS:** ON, operating in **linear/triode** region.
- **PMOS:** OFF (cutoff), because $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{in} < |V_{Tp}|$.
- **Output:** Pulled down to GND through the conducting NMOS. Hence $V_{out} = V_{OL} \approx 0$ V.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is there no static current?**
> In Regions 1 and 3, one transistor is in cutoff (OFF), so the current through the inverter is essentially the **leakage current (pA–nA range)**. Only in Region 2 (during switching) does a significant current flow, and this happens for a very short duration. This is why CMOS power dissipation is dominated by **dynamic (switching) power**, not static power.

## 2.2 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) and Key Parameters

The **VTC** is a plot of $V_{out}$ versus $V_{in}$, and is the most important static characteristic of the inverter. From the VTC, four critical voltages are defined:

- **$V_{OH}$ (Output High Voltage):** The minimum output voltage when output is logic HIGH. For CMOS, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.
- **$V_{OL}$ (Output Low Voltage):** The maximum output voltage when output is logic LOW. For CMOS, $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V.
- **$V_{IH}$ (Input High Voltage):** The minimum input voltage that is reliably recognized as logic HIGH.
- **$V_{IL}$ (Input Low Voltage):** The maximum input voltage that is reliably recognized as logic LOW.
- **$V_M$ (Switching Threshold):** The point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$.

### Noise Margins — The "Safety Zone" for Digital Signals

The **Noise Margins** quantify how much noise a digital signal can tolerate without causing a logic error at the input of the next gate.

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Higher noise margins mean **greater immunity to electrical noise**, which is critical in real-world digital systems exposed to EMI, crosstalk, and ground bounce. CMOS offers excellent noise margins (typically **0.3 V to 0.45 × V_DD**), making it the technology of choice for modern ICs.

## 2.3 Power Dissipation in CMOS

The **total power dissipation** in a CMOS inverter is the sum of three components:

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic} + P_{short-circuit}$$

- **$P_{static}$:** Power dissipated due to leakage current in steady state. Negligible in ideal CMOS ($P_{static} \approx 0$).
- **$P_{dynamic}$:** Power dissipated due to charging and discharging the **load capacitance $C_L$** at the output node. This is the **dominant** component.
- **$P_{short-circuit}$:** Power dissipated during switching when both PMOS and NMOS are simultaneously ON and a direct current path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

The **dynamic power** (the most important KTU formula) is:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **activity factor** (fraction of clock cycles during which the gate switches),
- $C_L$ = **load capacitance** at the output node,
- $V_{DD}$ = **supply voltage**,
- $f$ = **clock frequency** (switching frequency).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The $V_{DD}^2$ dependence is why **low-power design** focuses so heavily on **voltage scaling** — reducing $V_{DD}$ from 5 V to 3.3 V cuts dynamic power by more than **half**!

## 2.4 Propagation Delay

The **propagation delay** $t_p$ of a CMOS inverter is the time taken for the output to transition between 50% points of the input and output waveforms. It is governed by the **RC time constant** of the equivalent ON-resistance of the transistor and the load capacitance:

$$t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot R_n \cdot \ln 2}{1} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L$$

$$t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L \cdot R_p \cdot \ln 2}{1} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L$$

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$$

where $R_n$ and $R_p$ are the equivalent ON-resistances of the NMOS and PMOS transistors, respectively.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---------:|---|---|---|
| 1 | NMOS ON condition | $V_{GS} > V_{Tn}$ | Volts; $V_{Tn} > 0$ |
| 2 | PMOS ON condition | $V_{SG} > \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | Volts; $V_{Tp} < 0$ |
| 3 | Switching threshold | $V_M = \dfrac{r \cdot V_{DD}}{1 + r}$, where $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}$ | Volts; $k = \mu C_{ox} (W/L)$ |
| 4 | Noise margin HIGH | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Volts |
| 5 | Noise margin LOW | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Dynamic power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Watts |
| 7 | Static power (ideal CMOS) | $P_{stat} \approx 0$ | Watts |
| 8 | Short-circuit power | $P_{SC} \approx \dfrac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | Watts (advanced topic) |
| 9 | Propagation delay | $t_p = 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$ | Seconds |
| 10 | Energy per transition | $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars `|x|` have been written using `\vert` in the LaTeX expressions above to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do not use raw `|` inside table cells.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CMOS inverter and its derivatives (NAND, NOR) form the foundation of every digital IC manufactured today — from the **microprocessor** in your laptop to the **microcontroller** in a washing machine. The key engineering advantages are:

- **Ultra-low static power** → ideal for **battery-powered** IoT and mobile devices.
- **High noise immunity** → reliable operation in industrial environments.
- **Scalability** → transistor dimensions can be shrunk to **nanometer levels** (currently ~3 nm in advanced fabs).
- **High packing density** → billions of transistors on a single chip.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$

The **switching threshold $V_M$** is the point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out}$. At this point, both the NMOS and PMOS are operating in the **saturation region**, and the current flowing through the PMOS (from $V_{DD}$ to output) exactly equals the current flowing through the NMOS (from output to GND).

### Step 1: Write the saturation-region drain current equations.

For the **NMOS** in saturation:

$$I_{Dn,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$

For the **PMOS** in saturation:

$$I_{Dp,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

### Step 2: Apply the current continuity condition at the output node.

By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the output node in steady state, the PMOS current (flowing in) must equal the NMOS current (flowing out):

$$I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

### Step 3: Simplify the equation.

The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels from both sides. Take the **square root of both sides** (both currents are positive, so the principal root is valid):

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot (V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

### Step 4: Define the transconductance ratio $r$.

Let $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}$. Then $\sqrt{k_p} = r \cdot \sqrt{k_n}$. Substituting:

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot (V_M - V_{Tn}) = r \cdot \sqrt{k_n} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

Cancel $\sqrt{k_n}$ from both sides:

$$(V_M - V_{Tn}) = r \cdot (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

### Step 5: Solve for $V_M$.

Expand the right-hand side:

$$V_M - V_{Tn} = r \cdot V_{DD} - r \cdot V_M - r \cdot \vert V_{Tp} \vert$$

Group the $V_M$ terms on the left:

$$V_M + r \cdot V_M = r \cdot V_{DD} - r \cdot \vert V_{Tp} \vert + V_{Tn}$$

$$V_M (1 + r) = r \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}$$

$$\boxed{V_M = \frac{r \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}}$$

### Step 6: Special case — symmetric inverter.

For a **symmetric CMOS inverter** where $k_n = k_p$ (i.e., $r = 1$) and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert = V_T$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

This is the **ideal switching threshold**, and it is achieved by **sizing the PMOS wider than the NMOS** (typically $(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$) to compensate for the lower hole mobility.

## 3.2 Derivation of Dynamic Power Dissipation

The dynamic power dissipation arises from the charging and discharging of the **load capacitance $C_L$** at the output node.

### Step 1: Energy drawn from the supply during charging.

When the output switches from LOW to HIGH, the load capacitor charges from 0 to $V_{DD}$. The charge supplied is:

$$Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{supply} = Q \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 2: Energy stored in the capacitor.

The energy actually stored in the capacitor at the end of charging is:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 3: Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS.

The difference is dissipated in the **ON-resistance of the PMOS** during charging:

$$E_{PMOS} = E_{supply} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 4: Energy dissipated during discharging.

When the output switches from HIGH to LOW, the capacitor discharges through the NMOS. All the stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat in the NMOS:

$$E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 5: Total energy per switching cycle.

$$E_{total} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

### Step 6: Total dynamic power.

If the gate switches $f$ times per second (i.e., at switching frequency $f$), and only a fraction $\alpha$ of all clock cycles result in an actual switching event, the average dynamic power is:

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** Step 3 reveals a beautiful symmetry: **half** the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ is stored in the capacitor, and the other **half** is dissipated in the PMOS. The same half is then dissipated in the NMOS during discharge. So for **every switching transition**, the energy **$C_L V_{DD}^2$** is converted to heat, regardless of which direction the transition occurs. This is the fundamental reason why power scales quadratically with $V_{DD}$.

## 3.3 CMOS NAND Gate — Transistor Sizing Analysis

For a 2-input CMOS NAND gate, the **PMOS transistors are in parallel** and the **NMOS transistors are in series**.

### Step 3.3.1: Worst-case ON-resistance for pull-up.

When **one** input is LOW, the corresponding PMOS turns ON, and the PUN pulls the output HIGH through a **single PMOS**. So the worst-case pull-up resistance is:

$$R_{pu,NAND} = R_p$$

### Step 3.3.2: Worst-case ON-resistance for pull-down.

When **both** inputs are HIGH, both NMOS transistors are ON in series. The worst-case pull-down resistance is:

$$R_{pd,NAND} = 2 \cdot R_n$$

### Step 3.3.3: Sizing for symmetric switching.

To make $V_M \approx V_{DD}/2$, we need the pull-up and pull-down strengths to be equal in the worst case:

$$R_p = 2 \cdot R_n$$

Since $R \propto \dfrac{1}{k} \propto \dfrac{1}{(W/L)}$, this requires:

$$\frac{W_p}{L_p} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W_n}{L_n}$$

But due to the lower mobility of holes ($\mu_p \approx 0.4 \mu_n$), a common practical sizing is:

$$(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$$

## 3.4 CMOS NOR Gate — Transistor Sizing Analysis

For a 2-input CMOS NOR gate, the **PMOS transistors are in series** and the **NMOS transistors are in parallel**.

### Step 3.4.1: Worst-case pull-up.

When **both** inputs are LOW, both PMOS turn ON in series:

$$R_{pu,NOR} = 2 \cdot R_p$$

### Step 3.4.2: Worst-case pull-down.

When **one** input is HIGH, the corresponding NMOS turns ON, and the PDN pulls the output LOW through a **single NMOS**:

$$R_{pd,NOR} = R_n$$

### Step 3.4.3: Sizing for symmetric switching.

$$2 \cdot R_p = R_n$$

So the NMOS must be made **wider** relative to PMOS to compensate. The same practical rule applies:

$$(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$$

## 3.5 Comparative Summary Table

| **Parameter** | **CMOS Inverter** | **2-Input NAND** | **2-Input NOR** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of PMOS | 1 | 2 (parallel) | 2 (series) |
| Number of NMOS | 1 | 2 (series) | 2 (parallel) |
| PUN topology | Single PMOS | Parallel | Series |
| PDN topology | Single NMOS | Series | Parallel |
| Output HIGH condition | $V_{in} = 0$ | Either input = 0 | Both inputs = 0 |
| Output LOW condition | $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ | Both inputs = $V_{DD}$ | Either input = $V_{DD}$ |
| Worst-case $R_{pu}$ | $R_p$ | $R_p$ | $2 R_p$ |
| Worst-case $R_{pd}$ | $R_n$ | $2 R_n$ | $R_n$ |
| Preferred for VLSI? | Yes (universal) | Yes (preferred) | No (slower) |
| Reason | — | Faster pull-up | Slower pull-up due to series PMOS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In real VLSI design, **NAND gates are preferred over NOR gates** because the series PMOS in NOR significantly degrades the pull-up drive strength, slowing the gate. NAND is the **workhorse** of CMOS digital design.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Circuit Schematic and Operation

The following Mermaid block represents the circuit topology of a standard CMOS inverter. Note that the V_DD rail and GND rail are at the top and bottom respectively, with the PMOS forming the pull-up and the NMOS forming the pull-down.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVDD["V_DD SUPPLY RAIL"]
    nodePMOS["PMOS TRANSISTOR P1\nSource at V_DD\nGate tied to Input\nDrain tied to Output"]
    nodeOUT["OUTPUT NODE V_OUT"]
    nodeNMOS["NMOS TRANSISTOR N1\nDrain tied to Output\nGate tied to Input\nSource at GND"]
    nodeGND["GROUND RAIL V_SS"]
    nodeIN["INPUT V_IN"]
    
    nodeVDD --> nodePMOS
    nodePMOS --> nodeOUT
    nodeOUT --> nodeNMOS
    nodeNMOS --> nodeGND
    nodeIN -.controls gate.-> nodePMOS
    nodeIN -.controls gate.-> nodeNMOS
    
    style nodeVDD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style nodeGND fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333,color:#000
    style nodeOUT fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    style nodeIN fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000
    style nodePMOS fill:#ffddcc,stroke:#993300,color:#000
    style nodeNMOS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

### Inverter Operation Truth Table (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stateLow["V_IN = 0 LOW\nPMOS = ON\nNMOS = OFF\nV_OUT = V_DD HIGH"]
    stateHigh["V_IN = V_DD HIGH\nPMOS = OFF\nNMOS = ON\nV_OUT = 0 LOW"]
    
    stateLow -->|Input rises above V_Tn| stateHigh
    stateHigh -->|Input falls below V_DD minus V_Tp| stateLow
    
    style stateLow fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style stateHigh fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a CMOS inverter. Mention any two advantages of CMOS over TTL.

**Model Answer:**

A CMOS inverter consists of one **PMOS transistor (P1)** as the pull-up network and one **NMOS transistor (N1)** as the pull-down network, connected in a complementary (push-pull) configuration between **V_DD** and **GND**. The gates of both transistors are tied together to form the input, and their drains are tied together to form the output.

**Operation:**
- When $V_{in} = 0$ V (LOW): The PMOS is ON (because $V_{SG} = V_{DD} > |V_{Tp}|$) and the NMOS is OFF (because $V_{GS} = 0 < V_{Tn}$). The output is pulled up to $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS, giving $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$.
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$ (HIGH): The PMOS is OFF and the NMOS is ON. The output is pulled down to GND, giving $V_{out} = V_{OL} \approx 0$ V.

**Two Advantages of CMOS over TTL:**
1. **Near-zero static power dissipation** — because no direct current path exists between V_DD and GND in steady state.
2. **High noise immunity** — large noise margins (typically 30–45% of V_DD).
3. **Higher packing density** — MOS transistors occupy much smaller silicon area than bipolar transistors.

*(Any two valid points accepted; diagram must be clearly labeled with V_DD, GND, PMOS, NMOS, V_in, V_out.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Differentiate between CMOS NAND and CMOS NOR gates in terms of transistor arrangement and logic function.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **CMOS NAND Gate** | **CMOS NOR Gate** |
|---|---|---|
| PMOS arrangement (PUN) | **Parallel** | **Series** |
| NMOS arrangement (PDN) | **Series** | **Parallel** |
| Output is LOW when | **Both** inputs are HIGH | **Any one** input is HIGH |
| Output is HIGH when | **At least one** input is LOW | **Both** inputs are LOW |
| Boolean function | $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | $Y = \overline{A + B}$ |
| Speed in VLSI | **Faster** (preferred) | **Slower** (PMOS in series) |

Both gates use 2 PMOS and 2 NMOS transistors (4 transistors total), but the topological arrangement is the exact dual of each other.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU ESE Pattern: Two questions with internal choice. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 4 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **CMOS inverter** and explain its operation in all three regions of the input voltage. Define the **switching threshold $V_M$** and derive its expression in terms of the transconductance ratio $r$ and supply voltage $V_{DD}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **2-input CMOS NAND gate**. Show that the output follows the NAND truth table for all four input combinations. Discuss **transistor sizing** issues for symmetric switching. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — CMOS Inverter Operation and $V_M$ Derivation

**Circuit Description:**

The CMOS inverter consists of:
- One **PMOS transistor (P1)** with source at V_DD, gate at V_in, drain at V_out.
- One **NMOS transistor (N1)** with source at GND, gate at V_in, drain at V_out.

**Operation in three regions:**

**Region 1: $0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$**
- NMOS: $V_{GS} = V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ → **NMOS is OFF (cutoff)**.
- PMOS: $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{in} > |V_{Tp}|$ → **PMOS is ON (linear region)**.
- **Output:** $V_{out} = V_{DD} = V_{OH}$.

**Region 2: $V_{Tn} \le V_{in} \le V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$**
- **Both NMOS and PMOS are ON, both operating in saturation.**
- High current flows momentarily from V_DD to GND (this is the short-circuit current during switching).
- **Output:** $V_{out}$ transitions sharply from HIGH to LOW; $V_{out} = V_{in}$ defines the switching threshold $V_M$.

**Region 3: $V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}| < V_{in} \le V_{DD}$**
- NMOS: $V_{GS} = V_{in} > V_{Tn}$ → **NMOS is ON (linear region)**.
- PMOS: $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - V_{in} < |V_{Tp}|$ → **PMOS is OFF (cutoff)**.
- **Output:** $V_{out} = 0 = V_{OL}$.

**Derivation of Switching Threshold $V_M$:**

The switching threshold is the point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$. At this point, both transistors are in saturation, and the currents are equal.

$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$

$$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

Setting $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$ (KCL at output node):

$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$$

Taking the square root and defining $r = \sqrt{k_p / k_n}$:

$$(V_M - V_{Tn}) = r(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$$

Solving for $V_M$:

$$\boxed{V_M = \frac{r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}}$$

**Special Case:** For a symmetric inverter with $k_p = k_n$ and $V_{Tn} = |V_{Tp}|$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

---

#### Part (b) — CMOS NAND Gate Operation and Transistor Sizing

**Circuit Diagram Description:**

A 2-input CMOS NAND gate uses:
- **Two PMOS transistors (P1, P2) in PARALLEL** between V_DD and V_out.
- **Two NMOS transistors (N1, N2) in SERIES** between V_out and GND.
- Inputs: A → gates of P1 and N1; B → gates of P2 and N2.

**Truth Table Analysis:**

| **A** | **B** | **P1** | **P2** (PUN) | **N1** | **N2** (PDN) | **V_out** | **Logic** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | V_DD | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | V_DD | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | V_DD | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 0 | 0 |

**Observation:** Output is LOW **only** when both A = B = 1, confirming $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

**Transistor Sizing:**

The worst-case pull-down resistance (both NMOS ON in series) is:

$$R_{pd,wc} = 2 R_n$$

The worst-case pull-up resistance (one PMOS ON) is:

$$R_{pu,wc} = R_p$$

For symmetric switching ($V_M = V_{DD}/2$), we need $R_{pu,wc} = R_{pd,wc}$:

$$R_p = 2 R_n$$

Since $R \propto 1/(\mu \cdot W/L)$ and $\mu_p \approx 0.4 \mu_n$, the typical sizing rule is:

$$(W/L)_p \approx 2.5 \times (W/L)_n$$

This ensures that the PMOS provides equivalent drive strength despite lower hole mobility.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 4 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** of a CMOS inverter with a neat diagram. Define **$V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{IL}$**, and derive the expressions for **noise margins $NM_H$ and $NM_L$**. Discuss the factors that affect the shape of the VTC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the circuit and explain the operation of a **2-input CMOS NOR gate** for all four input combinations. Compare the **worst-case pull-up and pull-down resistances** of the CMOS NOR gate with those of the CMOS NAND gate. Explain why **NAND gates are preferred over NOR gates in VLSI design**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — VTC and Noise Margins

**Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC):**

The VTC is the plot of $V_{out}$ versus $V_{in}$ of a CMOS inverter. It is the most fundamental static characteristic used to evaluate the DC behavior of the gate.

**Description of the VTC Curve:**

1. **For $V_{in} < V_{Tn}$:** $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (output HIGH, flat region).
2. **For $V_{Tn} < V_{in} < V_M$:** $V_{out}$ begins to fall steeply; both transistors are in saturation.
3. **At $V_{in} = V_M$ (Switching Threshold):** $V_{out} = V_{in}$; the curve crosses the line $V_{out} = V_{in}$.
4. **For $V_M < V_{in} < V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$:** $V_{out}$ continues to fall sharply.
5. **For $V_{in} > V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$:** $V_{out} = V_{OL} \approx 0$ (output LOW, flat region).

The curve has a characteristic **sharp, almost rectangular** shape with a steep transition region, which is the hallmark of CMOS and the reason for its high noise immunity.

**Definitions:**

- $V_{OH}$ = Output HIGH voltage (typically $V_{DD}$).
- $V_{OL}$ = Output LOW voltage (typically 0 V).
- $V_{IH}$ = Minimum input voltage that is guaranteed to be recognized as a logic HIGH. Determined from the VTC as the point where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the lower transition.
- $V_{IL}$ = Maximum input voltage that is guaranteed to be recognized as a logic LOW. Determined from the VTC as the point where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ on the upper transition.

**Noise Margins:**

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$$

These represent the "safety zone" of voltages that can be added as noise to a logic signal without causing an erroneous logic transition at the input of the next gate.

**Factors Affecting VTC Shape:**

1. **Transconductance ratio $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$** — Increasing $k_p$ (wider PMOS) shifts $V_M$ upward.
2. **Threshold voltages $V_{Tn}$ and $V_{Tp}$** — Higher $V_{Tn}$ shifts the transition region to the right.
3. **Supply voltage $V_{DD}$** — A higher $V_{DD}$ widens the VTC and increases the noise margins.
4. **Load capacitance and transistor parasitic capacitances** — Affect the dynamic response, not the static VTC.

---

#### Part (b) — CMOS NOR Gate Operation and Comparative Analysis

**Circuit Diagram Description:**

A 2-input CMOS NOR gate uses:
- **Two PMOS transistors (P1, P2) in SERIES** between V_DD and V_out.
- **Two NMOS transistors (N1, N2) in PARALLEL** between V_out and GND.
- Inputs: A → gates of P1 and N1; B → gates of P2 and N2.

**Truth Table Analysis:**

| **A** | **B** | **P1** | **P2** (PUN) | **N1** | **N2** (PDN) | **V_out** | **Logic** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | V_DD | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 0 | 0 |

**Observation:** Output is HIGH **only** when both A = B = 0, confirming $Y = \overline{A + B}$.

**Comparative Resistance Analysis:**

| **Gate** | **Worst-case $R_{pu}$** | **Worst-case $R_{pd}$** | **Slowest transition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CMOS Inverter | $R_p$ | $R_n$ | Symmetric |
| CMOS NAND | $R_p$ (one PMOS ON) | $2 R_n$ (both NMOS ON in series) | **Pull-down (Falling edge)** |
| CMOS NOR | $2 R_p$ (both PMOS ON in series) | $R_n$ (one NMOS ON) | **Pull-up (Rising edge)** |

**Why NAND is Preferred over NOR in VLSI:**

1. **Series PMOS in NOR is more problematic than series NMOS in NAND.** This is because the **PMOS already has lower mobility** ($\mu_p \approx 0.4 \mu_n$). Putting two PMOS in series further halves the drive current, making the pull-up transition **quadratically slow** in W/L ratio.
2. **Series NMOS in NAND** is less critical because the NMOS is inherently faster. Although there are two NMOS in series, sizing them with larger W/L can easily compensate for the increased resistance.
3. **NAND gates also have lower input capacitance** per input pin compared to equivalent NOR implementations, making them better for driving high fan-out nets.
4. **Standard cell libraries** in industry (e.g., TSMC, Intel, Synopsys DesignWare) overwhelmingly use NAND-based complex gates (AOI, OAI) for high-performance paths.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the PUN and PDN topologies:** A very common error is to swap the PUN and PDN — i.e., drawing PMOS in series and NMOS in parallel for a NAND gate. **Marks are deducted heavily** if the transistor arrangement is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting to mark the bulk connections:** In CMOS schematics, the PMOS bulk (n-substrate) must be connected to V_DD, and the NMOS bulk (p-substrate) must be connected to GND. Examiners often look for this.
> 3. **Confusing absolute value notation in answers:** Always write $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ or $\lvert V_{Tp} \rvert$ (or state "magnitude of V_Tp") — never confuse sign conventions.
> 4. **Skipping the VTC derivation:** A 7-mark question on $V_M$ derivation **must include all six steps** shown in Section 3.1. Skipping the square root or the substitution of $r$ leads to loss of 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Static power assumption:** Do **not** write $P_{static} = 0$ without the qualifier "in ideal CMOS" or "ignoring leakage." In real nanoscale CMOS, leakage current is non-negligible.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the activity factor $\alpha$:** The dynamic power formula must include $\alpha$; writing $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ without $\alpha$ loses 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 4**

### 🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize

- **CMOS Inverter** = 1 PMOS (PUN) + 1 NMOS (PDN); complementary action; output is **inverse** of input.
- **CMOS NAND** = 2 PMOS in **parallel** (PUN) + 2 NMOS in **series** (PDN); $Y = \overline{AB}$.
- **CMOS NOR** = 2 PMOS in **series** (PUN) + 2 NMOS in **parallel** (PDN); $Y = \overline{A+B}$.
- **PMOS turns ON** when input is LOW (0); **NMOS turns ON** when input is HIGH (1).
- In **steady state**, one network is ON and the other is OFF → **zero static current path** from V_DD to GND.
- **Static power dissipation in ideal CMOS = 0**.
- **Dynamic power** is dominant: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.
- **Energy per switching transition** = $C_L V_{DD}^2$ (split equally: half dissipated in PMOS during charge, half in NMOS during discharge).

### 🔑 Formulas to Memorize (KTU 2024)

- **Switching threshold:** $V_M = \dfrac{r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}$, where $r = \sqrt{k_p / k_n}$.
- **Symmetric inverter condition:** $r = 1 \Rightarrow V_M = V_{DD}/2$.
- **Noise margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
- **Propagation delay:** $t_p = 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$.
- **Dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.

### 🔑 Topological Rules (Always Tested in KTU)

- **PUN rule:** Conducting PMOS → connect output to V_DD (logic 1).
- **PDN rule:** Conducting NMOS → connect output to GND (logic 0).
- **Duality rule:** PUN and PDN are duals — series ↔ parallel.
- **DeMorgan correspondence:** NAND structure = AND in PUN; NOR structure = OR in PUN.

### 🔑 Numerical Values to Remember

- For a 5 V CMOS inverter: $V_{OH} = 5$ V, $V_{OL} = 0$ V, $V_M \approx 2.5$ V.
- Typical noise margins: $NM_H \approx 1.5$ V, $NM_L \approx 1.5$ V (for 5 V supply).
- Hole mobility $\mu_p \approx 0.4 \mu_n$ → PMOS is sized $\approx 2.5 \times$ wider than NMOS for symmetric drive strength.

### 🔑 Common 1-Line Answers for Quick Recall

- **Why CMOS is preferred over TTL?** "Zero static power dissipation and high noise immunity."
- **Why NAND is preferred over NOR in CMOS?** "Series PMOS in NOR severely degrades pull-up drive strength due to lower hole mobility."
- **Why is there no static current in CMOS?** "In steady state, the PUN and PDN are never simultaneously conducting, so there is no direct path from V_DD to GND."
- **What is the switching threshold?** "The input voltage at which the output voltage equals the input voltage, $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$."
- **What is the dominant power dissipation mechanism in CMOS?** "Dynamic power due to charging and discharging of the load capacitance."

### 🔑 Last-Minute Strategy Tip for KTU Exam

> In the 14-mark questions, **always draw the circuit diagram first (3 marks secured)**, then proceed to the truth table (2 marks), then the operation explanation (4 marks), and finally the sizing/derivation part (5 marks). Even if you cannot complete the derivation, a clean circuit diagram and correct truth table will earn substantial partial credit.
